verses.flow: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Logos" (λόγος, Strong's G3056) carries a vast semantic range: word, reason, account, divine utterance. In Jewish context it echoes the creative "word of the Lord" (dabar YHWH) of Genesis 1 and Psalm 33:6. The preposition "pros" (πρός) with the accusative indicates face-to-face relational orientation, not merely spatial proximity—the Word was "toward" God in intimate communion. THEOLOGICAL: The anarthrous "theos" (θεός) in the final clause—"God was the Word"—is a qualitative predicate nominative: the Word possesses the full nature of deity without exhausting the person of the Father. This is the foundation of Trinitarian Christology.

verses.flow: This one was in the beginning with God.
All things through him came into being, and apart from him came into being not even one thing which has come into being.
verses.note: GREEK: "Egeneto" (ἐγένετο, from γίνομαι, Strong's G1096) means "came into being," distinguishing created things from the eternal Word who simply "was" (ἦν). The double negative "oude hen" (οὐδὲ ἕν) is emphatic: not even one single thing. THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes the Word as agent of all creation, echoing Colossians 1:16 and Hebrews 1:2. Nothing in the created order exists apart from the Word's agency.

verses.flow: In him was life, and the life was the light of men.
And the light in the darkness shines, and the darkness did not overcome it.
verses.note: GREEK: "Katelaben" (κατέλαβεν, from καταλαμβάνω, Strong's G2638) carries a deliberate double meaning: "comprehend/understand" and "overcome/seize." The darkness neither grasped the light intellectually nor overpowered it. The present tense "shines" (φαίνει) contrasts with the aorist "did not overcome," suggesting the light's shining is continuous and undefeated.

verses.flow: There came a man, having been sent from God, whose name was John.
This one came for a witness, that he might bear witness concerning the light, that all might believe through him.

verses.flow: The true light, which enlightens every man, was coming into the world.
He was in the world, and the world through him came into being, and the world did not know him.
He came unto his own things, and his own people did not receive him.

verses.flow: But as many as received him, to them he gave authority to become children of God, to those believing in his name,
who were born not of bloods, nor of the will of the flesh, nor of the will of a man, but of God.
verses.note: GREEK: The plural "bloods" (αἱμάτων, haimatōn) is unusual and likely refers to the mingling of bloodlines in human descent. The threefold negation—not of bloods, nor of fleshly will, nor of a man's will—emphatically excludes every form of natural human generation. THEOLOGICAL: Divine begetting stands in absolute contrast to human procreation; spiritual birth is solely the work of God (cf. John 3:3-8).

verses.flow: And the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we beheld his glory, glory as of the Only-Begotten from the Father, full of grace and truth.
verses.note: GREEK: "Eskēnōsen" (ἐσκήνωσεν, from σκηνόω, Strong's G4637) means "tabernacled" or "pitched his tent," a direct allusion to the Tabernacle (σκηνή) where God's glory-presence (Shekinah) dwelt among Israel (Exod 25:8-9; 40:34). "Monogenous" (μονογενοῦς, Strong's G3439) means "only-begotten" or "one-of-a-kind," denoting unique relationship to the Father. TYPOLOGY: The Incarnation fulfills the Tabernacle typology—the Glory that filled the tent of meeting now dwells in human flesh. The "grace and truth" (χάριτος καὶ ἀληθείας) echoes the Hebrew "chesed ve'emet" (steadfast love and faithfulness) of Exodus 34:6.

verses.flow: No one has ever seen God; the Only-Begotten God, the one being in the bosom of the Father, that one has made him known.
verses.note: GREEK: The textually significant reading "monogenēs theos" (μονογενὴς θεός, "Only-Begotten God") is attested in the earliest and best manuscripts (P66, P75, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus). "Exēgēsato" (ἐξηγήσατο, Strong's G1834) means "has made known, declared, interpreted"—from which English "exegesis" derives. The Son performs the exegesis of the invisible Father. THEOLOGICAL: This is among the highest Christological statements in the NT: the Son is himself God, uniquely positioned in the Father's bosom, and is the sole and definitive revelation of the unseen God.

verses.flow: And this is the witness of John, when the Jews sent from Jerusalem priests and Levites that they might ask him, "Who are you?"

verses.flow: He said, "I am a voice of one crying in the wilderness: 'Make straight the way of the Lord,' just as Isaiah the prophet said."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: John identifies himself with the voice of Isaiah 40:3 (LXX), the herald of YHWH's eschatological return to Zion. In the original Isaiah context, the "way of the Lord" refers to YHWH himself coming to his people—John's application of this to Jesus implicitly identifies Jesus with YHWH. HISTORICAL: The Qumran community (1QS 8:13-14) also applied Isaiah 40:3 to their wilderness preparation, showing this was a live messianic text in Second Temple Judaism.

verses.flow: On the next day he sees Jesus coming toward him and says, "Behold, the Lamb of God, the one taking away the sin of the world!

GREEK: "Airōn" (αἴρων, Strong's G142) means both "taking up/bearing" and "taking away/removing"—the Lamb both bears the sin and removes it. The singular "sin" (ἁμαρτίαν) treats the world's transgression as a unified reality that the Lamb addresses comprehensively.

verses.note: GREEK: Some manuscripts read "ho eklektos" (ὁ ἐκλεκτός, "the Chosen One," P5, P106, Sinaiticus) rather than "ho huios" (ὁ υἱός, "the Son"). TYPOLOGY: This title links Jesus to the Isaianic Servant figure and the messianic elect one of the divine council (cf. 1 Pet 2:4, 6). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The designation "Chosen One of God" positions Jesus as the one uniquely selected by God from among all beings—a concept resonant with the heavenly court imagery where God commissions his agent.

verses.flow: He first finds his own brother Simon and says to him, "We have found the Messiah" (which is translated, Christ).
verses.note: GREEK: "Messias" (Μεσσίας) is a Greek transliteration of the Aramaic "Meshicha" (מְשִׁיחָא), which John translates as "Christos" (Χριστός, "Anointed One"). This is one of only two NT occurrences of the transliterated Aramaic form (cf. John 4:25). HISTORICAL: First-century messianic expectation was diverse—ranging from a Davidic warrior-king to a priestly figure to a heavenly Son of Man—but the core concept was God's anointed eschatological agent who would restore Israel.

verses.flow: And he says to him, "Amen, amen, I say to you, you will see heaven opened and the angels of God ascending and descending upon the Son of Man."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse directly recalls Jacob's vision at Bethel (Gen 28:12), where angels ascended and descended on a ladder between heaven and earth. Jesus identifies himself as the true Bethel—the place where heaven and earth intersect, the living gate of God. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The angels of God ascending and descending upon the Son of Man depicts Jesus as the locus of divine council traffic between the heavenly and earthly realms. The "Son of Man" title echoes Daniel 7:13-14, where the "one like a son of man" approaches the Ancient of Days and receives everlasting dominion. THEOLOGICAL: The "amen, amen" (ἀμὴν ἀμήν) formula is unique to John's Gospel and functions as a solemn divine oath, asserting Jesus's supreme authority as the mediator between God and humanity.

verses.flow: And on the third day a wedding took place in Cana of Galilee, and the mother of Jesus was there.
verses.note: GREEK: "On the third day" (τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ) — counting from the sequence of days in John 1:29, 35, 43. The phrase carries forward John's careful chronological structuring of the opening week. TYPOLOGY: The "third day" foreshadows the resurrection (v.19-22) and echoes the theophany at Sinai, which occurred on the third day (Exod 19:11, 16). A wedding on the third day typologically anticipates the eschatological marriage feast of the Lamb (Rev 19:7-9).

verses.flow: And Jesus also was invited, and his disciples, to the wedding.

verses.flow: And when wine ran short, the mother of Jesus says to him, "They have no wine."
And Jesus says to her, "What is that to me and to you, woman? My hour has not yet come."
verses.note: GREEK: "What is that to me and to you?" (Τί ἐμοὶ καὶ σοί) is a Semitic idiom (cf. Judg 11:12 LXX; 2 Sam 16:10) expressing distance or disengagement. "Woman" (γύναι, vocative of γυνή, Strong's G1135) is not disrespectful but formal; Jesus uses the same address from the cross (John 19:26). THEOLOGICAL: "My hour" (ἡ ὥρα μου) is a major Johannine theme pointing to his glorification through death and resurrection (John 7:30; 8:20; 12:23; 13:1; 17:1).

verses.flow: His mother says to the servants, "Whatever he says to you, do it."

verses.flow: Now there were six stone water jars set there according to the purification rites of the Jews, each holding two or three measures.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The six stone jars held two to three "measures" (μετρητάς, metrētēs, Strong's G3355), each approximately 9 gallons, totaling 120-180 gallons. Stone vessels were preferred for Jewish purification rites because stone did not contract ritual uncleanness (m. Kelim 10.1). TYPOLOGY: The water of Jewish purification being transformed into wine signifies the old covenant order giving way to the abundance of the messianic age. Six, being one short of seven, may symbolize the incompleteness of the old order now fulfilled by Christ.

verses.flow: Jesus says to them, "Fill the water jars with water." And they filled them up to the brim.
And he says to them, "Draw out now and carry it to the master of the feast." And they carried it.

verses.flow: And when the master of the feast tasted the water that had become wine, and did not know whence it was — but the servants who had drawn the water knew — the master of the feast calls the bridegroom,
and says to him, "Every man sets out the good wine first, and when they have drunk freely, then the lesser; you have kept the good wine until now."
This beginning of signs Jesus did in Cana of Galilee, and he manifested his glory, and his disciples believed in him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Signs" (σημείων, from σημεῖον, Strong's G4592) is John's preferred term rather than the Synoptic "mighty works" (δυνάμεις). A sēmeion points beyond itself to a theological reality. THEOLOGICAL: "Manifested his glory" (ἐφανέρωσεν τὴν δόξαν αὐτοῦ) echoes the Prologue — "we beheld his glory" (John 1:14). The glory of the incarnate Word is revealed through signs, connecting Jesus to the divine kavod/doxa visible in the tabernacle and temple (Exod 40:34-35).

verses.flow: After this he went down to Capernaum, he and his mother and his brothers and his disciples, and there they remained not many days.

verses.flow: And to those selling the doves he said, "Take these things away from here; do not make the house of my Father a house of trade."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The house of my Father" (τὸν οἶκον τοῦ πατρός μου) is a direct claim of unique filial relationship to God. Jesus does not say "our Father" but "my Father," asserting divine sonship and authority over the temple itself. TYPOLOGY: The phrase "house of trade" (οἶκον ἐμπορίου) echoes Zechariah 14:21 LXX, where in the eschatological day "there shall no longer be a trader in the house of the Lord Almighty." Jesus acts as the eschatological Lord purifying his own temple (Mal 3:1-3).

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: His disciples remembered that it is written, "The zeal of your house will consume me."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Psalm 69:9 (LXX 68:10). The future tense "will consume me" (καταφάγεταί με) is significant — where the Hebrew psalm uses a past tense, John's citation follows a variant reading pointing prophetically forward to Jesus' death. Psalm 69 is one of the most frequently cited psalms in the NT as a psalm of the righteous sufferer (cf. John 15:25; 19:28-29; Rom 15:3).

verses.flow: The Jews therefore answered and said to him, "What sign do you show to us, seeing that you do these things?"
Jesus answered and said to them, "Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up."
verses.note: GREEK: "Destroy" (Λύσατε, aorist imperative of λύω, Strong's G3089) means literally "loose, dissolve, break down." "Temple" here is ναόν (naos, Strong's G3485), the inner sanctuary or shrine, not ἱερόν (hieron), the broader temple complex used in v.14-15. This distinction is theologically precise. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus identifies his body as the true naos — the dwelling place of God's presence. This is the ultimate fulfillment of tabernacle/temple theology: God dwells bodily in Christ (Col 2:9). The three-day raising is the clearest early prediction of the resurrection.

verses.flow: The Jews therefore said, "Forty-six years was this temple being built, and you will raise it up in three days?"
But he was speaking concerning the temple of his body.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: John provides the definitive interpretive key: "the temple of his body" (τοῦ ναοῦ τοῦ σώματος αὐτοῦ). The incarnate Christ is the true locus of divine presence, replacing the Jerusalem temple. This theme runs through John's Gospel (1:14 — "tabernacled among us") and into the Apocalypse where the Lamb is the temple of the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:22). DIVINE_COUNCIL: As the embodied divine presence, Jesus fulfills what the earthly temple only shadowed — the intersection of heaven and earth where God dwells among his people.

verses.flow: When therefore he was raised from the dead, his disciples remembered that he was saying this, and they believed the Scripture and the word which Jesus had spoken.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And when he was in Jerusalem at the Passover, during the feast, many believed in his name, beholding his signs which he was doing.
But Jesus himself was not entrusting himself to them, because he knew all men,
verses.note: GREEK: "Was not entrusting" (οὐκ ἐπίστευεν) uses the same verb πιστεύω (Strong's G4100) as the crowd's "believing" in v.23, creating a deliberate wordplay: they believed (ἐπίστευσαν) in him, but he did not believe/entrust (ἐπίστευεν) himself to them. THEOLOGICAL: This reveals Jesus' divine omniscience — he "knew all men" (γινώσκειν πάντας). Such comprehensive knowledge of the human heart is an attribute of God alone (1 Sam 16:7; 1 Kgs 8:39; Jer 17:10), underscoring John's high Christology throughout.

verses.flow: and because he had no need that anyone should bear witness concerning man, for he himself knew what was in man.
Now there was a man of the Pharisees, Nicodemus his name, a ruler of the Jews.
verses.note: GREEK: "Pharisaios" (Φαρισαῖος) designates the sect known for strict Torah observance. "Archōn" (ἄρχων, G758) means "ruler" or "chief," indicating Nicodemus held a seat on the Sanhedrin, the supreme Jewish council. HISTORICAL: As a member of the ruling council, Nicodemus represents the highest tier of Jewish religious authority engaging with Jesus.

verses.flow: This one came to him by night and said to him, "Rabbi, we know that from God you have come as a teacher, for no one is able to do these signs that you do unless God be with him."
Jesus answered and said to him, "Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born from above, he is not able to see the kingdom of God."
verses.note: GREEK: "Anōthen" (ἄνωθεν, G509) carries a deliberate double meaning: "from above" and "again/anew." John exploits this ambiguity throughout the passage—Jesus means "from above" (divine origin), while Nicodemus hears "again" (repetition). This wordplay is central to the misunderstanding motif in John's Gospel. THEOLOGICAL: Entry into the kingdom of God requires not human effort but a divine begetting—a birth whose origin is heavenly, not earthly.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Nicodemus says to him, "How is a man able to be born, being old? He is not able to enter into the womb of his mother a second time and be born?"
Jesus answered, "Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born of water and Spirit, he is not able to enter into the kingdom of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Born of water and Spirit" (ἐξ ὕδατος καὶ πνεύματος) has been interpreted variously as (1) natural birth and spiritual birth, (2) baptism and the Spirit, or (3) an echo of Ezekiel 36:25-27 where God promises to sprinkle clean water and put a new spirit within Israel. The Ezekiel background is most likely given Nicodemus's expected knowledge as a teacher of Israel (v.10). TYPOLOGY: The water-and-spirit language recalls the creation narrative (Gen 1:2) where the Spirit of God moved over the waters, suggesting new creation theology.

verses.flow: The wind blows where it wills, and you hear its voice, but you do not know from where it comes and where it goes. So is everyone who has been born of the Spirit."
verses.note: GREEK: "Pneuma" (πνεῦμα, G4151) means both "wind" and "spirit," creating another Johannine double meaning. Jesus uses the observable but mysterious movement of wind as an analogy for the sovereign, invisible work of the Spirit. The verb "pnei" (πνεῖ, "blows") shares the same root as pneuma, reinforcing the wordplay. This double sense is untranslatable in most languages without loss.

verses.flow: And no one has ascended into heaven except the one having descended out of heaven, the Son of Man.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse makes an extraordinary claim of heavenly preexistence for the Son of Man. "No one has ascended into heaven" challenges all human claims to direct heavenly knowledge, establishing Jesus as the sole authoritative revealer of divine things. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "Son of Man" title echoes Daniel 7:13-14, where a divine figure approaches the Ancient of Days and receives universal dominion—a figure understood in Second Temple Judaism as a heavenly, exalted being distinct from yet sharing in the authority of God.

verses.flow: And just as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The bronze serpent of Numbers 21:4-9 is here declared a type of Christ's crucifixion. As the Israelites who looked upon the lifted serpent were healed from death, so those who look in faith upon the lifted-up Son of Man receive eternal life. The verb "hypsoō" (ὑψόω, G5312) carries a double meaning in John: physical lifting up on the cross and exaltation to glory (cf. John 8:28; 12:32-34).

verses.flow: For thus God loved the world, that he gave the only-begotten Son, that everyone believing in him should not perish but have eternal life.
verses.note: GREEK: "Monogenēs" (μονογενής, G3439) means "only-begotten" or "one-of-a-kind, unique." It emphasizes the Son's singular, unrepeatable relationship to the Father. "Kosmos" (κόσμος, G2889) here refers to the created order of humanity in its fallen state—the scope of God's love is universal in its reach. THEOLOGICAL: This verse encapsulates the entire Gospel narrative: the Father's love as the originating cause, the giving of the Son as the means, and faith as the instrument by which perishing humanity receives eternal life.

verses.flow: For God did not send the Son into the world that he might judge the world, but that the world might be saved through him.

verses.flow: The one believing in him is not judged; but the one not believing has been judged already, because he has not believed in the name of the only-begotten Son of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The perfect tense "kekritai" (κέκριται, "has been judged already") indicates that judgment is not merely future but is a present reality determined by one's response to the Son. Unbelief is not a neutral position but a settled state of condemnation. The "name" (ónoma) of the only-begotten Son represents his full revealed identity and authority.

verses.flow: And this is the judgment: that the light has come into the world, and men loved the darkness rather than the light, for their works were evil.
verses.note: GREEK: "Krisis" (κρίσις, G2920) here means not the act of sentencing but the basis or ground of judgment—the criterion by which judgment operates. THEOLOGICAL: The light-darkness motif is central to John's Gospel (cf. John 1:4-5, 9). The judgment consists not in God arbitrarily condemning but in humanity's willful preference for darkness. The moral condition of one's works determines the direction of one's movement toward or away from the light.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The bridegroom imagery draws on the Old Testament portrayal of God as Israel's bridegroom (Isa 62:4-5; Hos 2:16-20). By identifying Jesus as the bridegroom, the Baptist implicitly attributes to Jesus a role that belongs to God himself. The "friend of the bridegroom" (philos tou nymphiou) was the "shoshbin" in Jewish wedding custom—the one who arranged the marriage and then stepped aside with joy when the bridegroom arrived. THEOLOGICAL: John's joy is fulfilled precisely in his own diminishment, modeling the posture of all true witness-bearing.

verses.flow: The one having received his witness has set his seal that God is true.
For the one whom God sent speaks the words of God, for he does not give the Spirit by measure.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ou gar ek metrou didōsin to pneuma" — "for not by measure does he give the Spirit." The absence of a specified subject has led to debate: does God give the Spirit without measure to Jesus, or does Jesus give the Spirit without measure? In context, God is the likely giver, and Jesus is the recipient who therefore speaks God's words with full, unlimited divine authority. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This unlimited bestowal of the Spirit sets Jesus apart from all prior prophets and spirit-bearers in Israel's history, who received the Spirit in measured, partial grants (cf. Num 11:17, 25; 2 Kgs 2:9).

verses.flow: The one believing in the Son has eternal life; but the one disobeying the Son shall not see life, but the wrath of God abides upon him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Apeithōn" (ἀπειθῶν, G544) means "disobeying, being rebellious"—stronger than mere unbelief (apistōn). It implies willful defiance, not merely intellectual doubt. THEOLOGICAL: The chapter ends with an absolute binary: eternal life or abiding wrath. The present tense "menei" (μένει, "abides/remains") indicates that God's wrath is not a future threat alone but a present, continuing reality resting upon the one who disobeys the Son. This forms an inclusio with v.16—the same Son given in love becomes the criterion of final destiny.

verses.flow: When therefore Jesus knew that the Pharisees had heard that Jesus was making and baptizing more disciples than John—

verses.note: GREEK: ἔδει (edei, from δέω, "it was necessary/binding") implies divine necessity, not merely geographic convenience. THEOLOGICAL: The divine compulsion (dei) to pass through Samaria signals God's redemptive purpose extending beyond ethnic Israel. Jesus' mission to the Samaritans is portrayed as part of the Father's sovereign plan, not incidental travel.

verses.flow: He comes therefore to a city of Samaria called Sychar, near the piece of land which Jacob gave to Joseph his son.

verses.flow: Now the well of Jacob was there. Jesus therefore, having been wearied from the journey, was sitting thus upon the well. It was about the sixth hour.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The well of Jacob (Gen 29:1-12) is a betrothal type-scene in Hebrew narrative — Moses met Zipporah at a well (Exod 2:15-21), Isaac's servant found Rebekah at a well (Gen 24). Jesus at Jacob's well with a woman echoes the bridegroom motif, pointing to Christ as the divine bridegroom of his people. GREEK: κεκοπιακώς (kekopiakōs, perfect participle of κοπιάω) emphasizes the completed state of exhaustion — a vivid portrait of the incarnate Word's genuine humanity. The "sixth hour" (noon by Jewish reckoning) intensifies the weariness motif.

verses.flow: A woman comes out of Samaria to draw water. Jesus says to her, "Give me to drink."
For his disciples had gone away into the city in order that they might buy food.

verses.flow: The Samaritan woman therefore says to him, "How is it that you, being a Jew, ask to drink from me, being a Samaritan woman?" For Jews have no dealings with Samaritans.

verses.flow: Jesus answered and said to her, "If you knew the gift of God, and who it is that says to you, 'Give me to drink,' you would have asked him, and he would have given you living water."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The gift of God" (τὴν δωρεὰν τοῦ θεοῦ) encompasses both the living water and the giver himself. Jesus positions himself as both the one who gives and the one who must be asked — identifying himself with the divine source. GREEK: ὕδωρ ζῶν (hydōr zōn, "living water") has a double meaning: in common usage it refers to flowing/spring water as opposed to cistern water, but theologically it evokes the life-giving Spirit (cf. Jer 2:13, Zech 14:8, Ezek 47:1-12).

verses.flow: The woman says to him, "Lord, you have nothing to draw with, and the well is deep. From where then do you have the living water?
Are you greater than our father Jacob, who gave us the well, and himself drank from it, and his sons, and his livestock?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus answered and said to her, "Everyone who drinks from this water will thirst again.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive singular neuter

verses.flow: But whoever drinks from the water which I shall give him shall never thirst unto the age, but the water which I shall give him will become in him a fountain of water springing up into eternal life."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The water Jesus gives becomes an internal, self-renewing fountain (πηγὴ ὕδατος ἁλλομένου) — not a static reservoir but a springing source. This anticipates the outpouring of the Holy Spirit (John 7:37-39). GREEK: ἁλλομένου (hallomenou, "springing/leaping," from ἅλλομαι) is used in Acts 3:8 of the healed lame man leaping — suggesting exuberant, irrepressible life. TYPOLOGY: The contrast between Jacob's well (finite, requiring labor) and Jesus' living water (infinite, freely given) marks the superiority of the new covenant to the old.

verses.flow: The woman says to him, "Lord, give me this water, so that I may not thirst, nor come here to draw."
He says to her, "Go, call your husband, and come here."
The woman answered and said to him, "I do not have a husband." Jesus says to her, "Well did you say, 'A husband I do not have.'"
"For you have had five husbands, and the one whom you now have is not your husband. This you have spoken truly."
The woman says to him, "Lord, I perceive that you are a prophet.
Our fathers worshipped on this mountain, and you say that in Jerusalem is the place where it is necessary to worship."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "This mountain" refers to Mount Gerizim, where the Samaritans had built a rival temple (destroyed by John Hyrcanus in 128 BC). The Samaritan-Jewish dispute over the proper place of worship was rooted in the schism following Solomon's kingdom (cf. 1 Kgs 12). The Samaritan Pentateuch preserves a command to build an altar on Gerizim (their reading of Deut 27:4). THEOLOGICAL: The woman's question frames worship as a matter of sacred geography; Jesus' answer will reframe it as a matter of Spirit and truth.

verses.flow: Jesus says to her, "Believe me, woman, that an hour is coming when neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem will you worship the Father.
You worship what you do not know; we worship what we know, for salvation is from the Jews.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Salvation is from the Jews" (ἡ σωτηρία ἐκ τῶν Ἰουδαίων ἐστίν) affirms the covenant priority of Israel as the lineage through which the Messiah comes (Rom 9:4-5). Jesus does not relativize the Jewish-Samaritan dispute — the Jews possess the fuller revelation — yet he simultaneously transcends it. This is not ethnic superiority but salvation-historical specificity.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: But an hour is coming, and now is, when the true worshippers will worship the Father in spirit and truth; for indeed the Father seeks such ones as those worshipping him.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: God is spirit, and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth."
verses.note: GREEK: πνεῦμα ὁ θεός (pneuma ho theos, "Spirit [is] God" or "God [is] spirit") — the anarthrous πνεῦμα is predicate, describing God's nature rather than identifying God as identical with spirit. THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most significant ontological statements about God in the New Testament. Worship "in spirit and truth" means worship empowered by the Holy Spirit and grounded in the revealed truth of God — not merely sincerity or inward feeling. DIVINE_COUNCIL: God as spirit transcends all localized, temple-bound worship, reconfiguring the sacred space from a geographic location to the Spirit-indwelt community.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The woman says to him, "I know that Messiah is coming, the one called Christ. When that one comes, he will declare all things to us."
verses.note: GREEK: Μεσσίας (Messias) is a Greek transliteration of the Hebrew/Aramaic משׁיח (māšîaḥ), appearing only here and in John 1:41 in the entire NT. John provides the Greek equivalent χριστός (Christos, "Anointed One") for his audience. HISTORICAL: The Samaritans awaited the Taheb ("Restorer"), a Moses-like prophet figure based on Deut 18:15-18 rather than a Davidic king. The woman's expectation that the Messiah would "declare all things" aligns with this prophetic-revelatory role.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus says to her, "I am he, the one speaking to you."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Ἐγώ εἰμι (Egō eimi, "I am") — Jesus' self-identification is the most explicit messianic declaration in the Gospels to this point. The phrase carries overtones of the divine self-revelation at the burning bush (Exod 3:14 LXX). That Jesus reveals himself first to a Samaritan woman — not the Jewish religious establishment — is a radical inversion of expected messianic disclosure. TYPOLOGY: As the prophet greater than Moses (Deut 18:15), Jesus fulfills the very expectation the Samaritan woman articulates.

verses.flow: And upon this his disciples came, and they were marveling that he was speaking with a woman. Yet no one said, "What do you seek?" or "Why do you speak with her?"
The woman therefore left her water jar and went away into the city, and says to the people,
"Come, see a man who told me all things that I did. Can this be the Christ?"
They went out of the city and were coming to him.

The disciples therefore were saying to one another, "No one brought him anything to eat, did he?"

verses.flow: Jesus says to them, "My food is that I should do the will of the one having sent me, and that I should complete his work.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' "food" (βρῶμα, brōma) is obedience to the Father's will and the completion (τελειόω, teleioō) of his work. This anticipates the τετέλεσται ("It is finished") of John 19:30. The same verb for "complete/perfect" frames Jesus' entire ministry as a single work given by the Father. GREEK: τελειώσω (teleiōsō, aorist subjunctive of τελειόω) connotes bringing to full completion or perfection, not merely finishing a task.

verses.flow: Do you not say, 'There are yet four months, and the harvest comes'? Behold, I say to you, lift up your eyes and behold the fields, that they are white for harvest already.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The harvest imagery draws on OT prophetic traditions of eschatological ingathering (Joel 3:13, Isa 27:12-13). The fields "white for harvest" likely refers to the approaching Samaritans — their white garments visible against the landscape — making the metaphor both literal and theological. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus collapses the expected timeline between sowing and reaping; the eschatological harvest is breaking into the present moment.

I sent you to reap that for which you have not labored. Others have labored, and you have entered into their labor."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Others have labored" likely refers to the OT prophets, Moses, and possibly John the Baptist who prepared the ground. TYPOLOGY: This sowing-reaping pattern reflects the larger biblical theme of one generation laboring for the benefit of the next in salvation history (cf. 1 Cor 3:6-8).

verses.flow: And from that city many of the Samaritans believed in him because of the word of the woman testifying, "He told me all things that I did."
When therefore the Samaritans came to him, they were asking him to remain with them, and he remained there two days.
And many more believed because of his word.
And they were saying to the woman, "No longer do we believe because of your speech, for we ourselves have heard and we know that this one is truly the Savior of the world."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Samaritans' confession — ὁ σωτὴρ τοῦ κόσμου ("the Savior of the world") — is extraordinary in its scope. "Savior" (σωτήρ) was applied to Caesar in Roman imperial cult; its application to Jesus by Samaritans (not Jews) anticipates the universal reach of the gospel. HISTORICAL: That the title comes from Samaritans, considered outsiders, prefigures the Gentile mission and the breaking down of ethnic barriers in Christ (Eph 2:14).

verses.flow: And after the two days he went out from there into Galilee.
For Jesus himself testified that a prophet in his own homeland has no honor.

verses.flow: When therefore he came into Galilee, the Galileans received him, having seen all things that he did in Jerusalem at the feast, for they also had gone to the feast.
He came therefore again to Cana of Galilee, where he had made the water wine. And there was a certain royal official whose son was sick in Capernaum.
verses.note: GREEK: βασιλικός (basilikos, "royal official," from βασιλεύς, "king") likely denotes an official in the service of Herod Antipas, tetrarch of Galilee. STRUCTURE: John explicitly links this to Cana, where Jesus performed his first sign (John 2:1-11). The "second sign" notation (v.54) frames a literary unit: the first sign transformed water to wine (creation abundance), the second heals at a distance (sovereign authority over life and death).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: This one, having heard that Jesus had come out of Judea into Galilee, went to him and was asking that he would come down and heal his son, for he was about to die.
Jesus therefore said to him, "Unless you see signs and wonders, you will by no means believe."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' rebuke uses the second person plural (ἴδητε, πιστεύσητε) — he addresses not just the official but the broader Galilean populace. Their faith is contingent on signs and wonders (σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα), a phrase echoing Deuteronomy 13:1-3 where signs alone are insufficient grounds for faith. Jesus calls for faith grounded in his word, not spectacle.

verses.flow: The royal official says to him, "Lord, come down before my little child dies."
Jesus says to him, "Go; your son lives." The man believed the word which Jesus spoke to him, and was going on his way.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Your son lives" (ὁ υἱός σου ζῇ) — the man believes the bare word of Jesus without any visible confirmation, exemplifying the faith Jesus commended. This contrasts with the sign-dependent faith rebuked in v.48. The progression — from request, to rebuke, to word, to faith, to confirmation — models the movement from sign-faith to word-faith. GREEK: ἐπίστευσεν (episteusen, aorist of πιστεύω) with the dative τῷ λόγῳ indicates trust placed specifically in the spoken word.

verses.flow: And already as he was going down, his servants met him, saying that his boy lives.
He inquired therefore of them the hour in which he began to amend. They said therefore to him, "Yesterday at the seventh hour the fever left him."
The father therefore knew that it was in that hour in which Jesus said to him, "Your son lives." And he himself believed, and his whole household.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now this again was a second sign Jesus did, having come out of Judea into Galilee.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: John counts this as the "second sign" (δεύτερον σημεῖον), linking it explicitly to the first sign at Cana (John 2:11). Both signs involve Cana as the point of origin, both demonstrate Jesus' sovereign authority, and both produce faith. The enumeration suggests John is structuring his narrative around selected signs that reveal Jesus' glory (cf. John 20:30-31). TYPOLOGY: The healing at a distance — Jesus' word alone effecting life where death threatened — prefigures the ultimate life-giving word of resurrection (John 5:25, 11:43).

verses.flow: After these things there was a feast of the Jews, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.

verses.flow: Now there is in Jerusalem, by the Sheep Gate, a pool, which is called in Hebrew Bethesda, having five porticoes.
verses.note: GREEK: κολυμβήθρα (kolymbēthra, "pool," Strong's G2861) is a relatively rare term denoting a swimming pool or bathing pool. HISTORICAL: The pool of Bethesda (Βηθεσδά, "House of Mercy" or "House of Outpouring") has been archaeologically confirmed near the Sheep Gate in Jerusalem's northeast quarter, revealing a double pool with five covered colonnades matching John's description precisely. This detail attests to the author's firsthand knowledge of pre-70 AD Jerusalem.

verses.flow: In these lay a multitude of those who were sick—blind, lame, withered.

verses.flow: Jesus, seeing this man lying down and knowing that he had already been a long time thus, says to him, "Do you desire to become well?"

verses.flow: The sick man answered him, "Lord, I have no man, that when the water is stirred he might cast me into the pool; but while I am coming, another goes down before me."
Jesus says to him, "Rise, take up your mat and walk."
And immediately the man became well, and he took up his mat and walked. Now it was a Sabbath on that day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: John deliberately notes the Sabbath setting, establishing the conflict that drives the entire discourse. Jesus' command to carry the mat was understood as violating Sabbath halakah (cf. Jer 17:21-22; Neh 13:19). The healing itself inaugurates the extended controversy over Jesus' divine authority.

verses.flow: The Jews therefore were saying to the one who had been healed, "It is the Sabbath, and it is not lawful for you to take up your mat."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But he answered them, "The one who made me well, that one said to me, 'Take up your mat and walk.'"

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: They asked him, "Who is the man who said to you, 'Take up and walk'?"

verses.flow: But the one who had been healed did not know who it was, for Jesus had withdrawn, a crowd being in the place.
After these things Jesus finds him in the temple and said to him, "Behold, you have become well; sin no longer, lest something worse befall you."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The man went away and reported to the Jews that it is Jesus who made him well.
And on account of this the Jews were persecuting Jesus, because he was doing these things on the Sabbath.
But he answered them, "My Father is working until now, and I am working."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ὁ πατήρ μου ἕως ἄρτι ἐργάζεται κἀγὼ ἐργάζομαι—"My Father is working until now, and I am working." This is a profound Christological claim. Jewish theology acknowledged that God continued to sustain creation even on the Sabbath (cf. Gen 2:2-3 with Isa 40:28). By placing his own work in direct parallel with the Father's ongoing creative and sustaining activity, Jesus claims participation in the divine prerogative. GREEK: ἐργάζεται (ergazetai) is present middle indicative, emphasizing continuous, ongoing action.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: On account of this therefore the Jews were seeking all the more to kill him, because he was not only breaking the Sabbath, but was also calling God his own Father, making himself equal to God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Judean authorities correctly perceive the implications of Jesus' words: calling God πατέρα ἴδιον (patera idion, "his own Father") with the unique possessive ἴδιον implies a relationship distinct from Israel's corporate filial relationship with God. The phrase ἴσον ἑαυτὸν ποιῶν τῷ θεῷ ("making himself equal to God") uses ἴσος (isos, Strong's G2470), meaning "equal in quality, quantity, or dignity." DIVINE_COUNCIL: This verse is critical for Christology: the claim is not merely to a subordinate divine status but to equality with God himself.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus therefore answered and was saying to them, "Amen, amen, I say to you, the Son is not able to do anything from himself unless he sees the Father doing it; for whatever things that One does, these things also the Son does likewise.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' response does not deny the charge of equality but reframes it in terms of perfect unity of will and action between Father and Son. The Son does nothing ἀφ' ἑαυτοῦ ("from himself") independently—not from inability but from the perfect relational unity of the Godhead. STRUCTURE: This verse begins the great discourse on the Son's authority (vv. 19-30), structured around the themes of life-giving power and judicial authority.

verses.flow: For the Father loves the Son and shows him all things that he himself does; and greater works than these he will show him, so that you may marvel.
For just as the Father raises the dead and gives them life, so also the Son gives life to whom he wills.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ζῳοποιεῖ (zōopoiei, "gives life," Strong's G2227) is attributed equally to Father and Son. In Jewish theology, the power to resurrect the dead belongs to God alone (Deut 32:39; 1 Sam 2:6; 2 Kgs 5:7). Jesus' claim to give life "to whom he wills" (οὓς θέλει) asserts sovereign divine prerogative. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Life-giving power is the exclusive domain of the Creator; the Son exercises this without delegation or limitation, only by his own will.

verses.flow: For neither does the Father judge anyone, but he has given all judgment to the Son,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: so that all may honor the Son just as they honor the Father. The one who does not honor the Son does not honor the Father who sent him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The purpose (ἵνα) of the transfer of all judgment to the Son is that the Son receive equal honor (τιμή, timē) with the Father. This is an extraordinary claim: to dishonor the Son is to dishonor the Father. In a monotheistic framework, this demands that the Son share the divine identity. Cf. Isa 42:8, where God declares he will not give his glory to another—yet here the Father commands equal honor for the Son.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: Amen, amen, I say to you that the one who hears my word and believes the one who sent me has eternal life, and does not come into judgment, but has passed out of death into life.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most theologically dense verses in John. μεταβέβηκεν (metabebēken, perfect active indicative of μεταβαίνω) indicates a completed transition—the believer has already passed from death into life. Eternal life is presented as a present possession, not merely a future hope. GREEK: The perfect tense emphasizes the abiding result of a completed action: the crossing from the realm of death to the realm of life is an accomplished fact for the one who hears and believes.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Amen, amen, I say to you that an hour is coming, and now is, when the dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God, and those who hear shall live.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God and those who hear shall live"—this operates on two levels: spiritual resurrection in the present age ("and now is") and physical resurrection at the eschaton (vv. 28-29). The title "Son of God" (τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ) is used here with full theological weight, connecting the life-giving voice to divine identity.

verses.flow: For just as the Father has life in himself, so also he has given to the Son to have life in himself.
And he has given him authority to execute judgment, because he is the Son of Man.
verses.note: GREEK: υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου (huios anthrōpou, "Son of Man") appears here without the article, which is unusual and may emphasize qualitative force—"because he is Son-of-Man in character." The contrast between aorist (past, completed action of Moses) and present (ongoing, current giving by the Father) is theologically significant—the Father's provision is active and present in Jesus.

verses.flow: Jesus therefore answered them and said, "My teaching is not mine, but of the one who sent me.
If anyone wills to do his will, he shall know concerning the teaching, whether it is from God, or whether I speak from myself.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes an epistemological principle: obedient will precedes doctrinal certainty. The one who wills (θέλῃ, subjunctive of θέλω) to do God's will shall experientially know (γνώσεται, future of γινώσκω) the divine origin of Jesus's teaching. Knowledge of God is morally conditioned.

verses.flow: The one speaking from himself seeks his own glory; but the one seeking the glory of the one who sent him, this one is true, and unrighteousness is not in him.
Has not Moses given you the law? And none of you does the law. Why do you seek to kill me?"
The crowd answered, "You have a demon! Who seeks to kill you?"
Jesus answered and said to them, "One work I did, and you all marvel.
On account of this, Moses has given you circumcision—not that it is from Moses, but from the fathers—and on a Sabbath you circumcise a man.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Circumcision was given to Abraham (Gen 17:10-14) before Moses, hence Jesus's parenthetical correction "not that it is from Moses, but from the fathers." THEOLOGICAL: Jesus argues from lesser to greater (qal wahomer): if circumcision of one member is lawful on the Sabbath, how much more the healing of a whole man (v.23).

verses.flow: If a man receives circumcision on a Sabbath so that the law of Moses may not be broken, are you angry with me because I made a whole man well on a Sabbath?
Do not judge according to appearance, but judge with righteous judgment."
Therefore some of the people of Jerusalem were saying, "Is not this the one whom they seek to kill?
And behold, he speaks with boldness, and they say nothing to him. Can it be that the rulers have truly come to know that this is the Christ?
But this one—we know where he is from. But the Christ, whenever he comes, no one knows where he is from."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: A popular belief held that the Messiah would appear suddenly and mysteriously, perhaps based on Mal 3:1 ("suddenly the Lord whom you seek will come to his temple"). The irony in John is profound: they think they know Jesus's origin (Nazareth), but they do not know his true origin—from the Father (v.29). TYPOLOGY: The hidden-then-revealed Messiah pattern echoes Joseph's concealed identity before his brothers (Gen 42-45).

verses.flow: Therefore Jesus cried out in the temple, teaching and saying, "You both know me and you know where I am from. And I have not come from myself, but the one who sent me is true, whom you do not know.
verses.note: GREEK: ἔκραξεν (ekraxen, aorist of κράζω) indicates a loud, authoritative cry—a prophetic declaration in the temple precincts. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus simultaneously affirms and denies their knowledge: they know his earthly identity but are ignorant of his divine sending. "The one who sent me is true" (ἀληθινός, alēthinos) uses the word for genuinely real, not merely truthful—pointing to the Father's ultimate reality.

verses.flow: I know him, because I am from him, and he sent me."
Therefore they were seeking to seize him, and yet no one laid a hand upon him, because his hour had not yet come.
But many from the crowd believed in him and were saying, "When the Christ comes, will he do more signs than what this one has done?"

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: The Pharisees heard the crowd murmuring these things concerning him, and the chief priests and the Pharisees sent officers that they might seize him.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore Jesus said, "Yet a little time I am with you, and I go to the one who sent me.
You shall seek me and shall not find me, and where I am, you are not able to come."
The Jews therefore said to one another, "Where is this one about to go that we shall not find him? Is he about to go to the Dispersion among the Greeks and teach the Greeks?
What is this word that he said: 'You shall seek me and shall not find me, and where I am, you are not able to come'?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now on the last day, the great day of the feast, Jesus stood and cried out, saying, "If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and let him drink.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: On the last great day (Hoshana Rabbah, the seventh day, or possibly Shemini Atzeret, the eighth day), the priest would pour water from the Pool of Siloam upon the altar while the Hallel was sung—commemorating the wilderness rock (Exod 17:6) and anticipating eschatological blessing (Zech 14:8; Ezek 47:1-12). TYPOLOGY: Jesus identifies himself as the source of living water, fulfilling the rock in the wilderness (1 Cor 10:4) and the eschatological temple stream.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular neuter

verses.flow: The one believing in me, as the Scripture said, rivers of living water shall flow out of his innermost being."
verses.note: GREEK: κοιλία (koilia) literally means "belly" or "innermost part"—here metaphorical for the deepest interior of a person. ῥεύσουσιν (rheusousin, future of ῥέω) means "shall flow." The Scripture reference likely alludes to Isa 55:1, Isa 58:11, Ezek 47:1-12, or Zech 14:8. TYPOLOGY: The rivers of living water flowing from the believer echo the temple-river vision of Ezekiel 47, with believers themselves becoming temples of the Spirit (1 Cor 6:19).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Now this he said concerning the Spirit, whom those having believed in him were about to receive; for the Spirit was not yet given, because Jesus was not yet glorified.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: John provides the authoritative interpretive key: the living water is the Holy Spirit (πνεῦμα, pneuma). "The Spirit was not yet" (οὔπω γὰρ ἦν πνεῦμα) does not mean the Spirit did not exist but that the Spirit had not yet been given in the new-covenant fullness, which awaited Jesus's glorification through death, resurrection, and ascension (Acts 2:33). STRUCTURE: This editorial comment links John 7 to John 14-16 (Paraclete promises) and John 20:22.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore some from the crowd, having heard these words, were saying, "This is truly the Prophet."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Prophet" (ὁ προφήτης) refers to the eschatological prophet like Moses promised in Deut 18:15-18. Some distinguished this figure from the Christ (v.41), while others identified them. TYPOLOGY: Jesus fulfills both roles—the prophet greater than Moses who speaks God's words, and the anointed Davidic King.

verses.flow: Others were saying, "This is the Christ." But others were saying, "Surely the Christ does not come from Galilee, does he?
Has not the Scripture said that the Christ comes from the seed of David, and from Bethlehem, the village where David was?"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The crowd unknowingly testifies to Jesus's messianic credentials. The Scripture they cite (Mic 5:2; 2 Sam 7:12) about Davidic descent and Bethlehem birth is precisely fulfilled in Jesus, though the crowd assumes he is merely from Galilee. THEOLOGICAL: John's dramatic irony: the very objection raised against Jesus actually confirms his identity. The reader knows from the Synoptics (Matt 2:1; Luke 2:4-7) what the crowd does not.

verses.flow: Therefore a division arose in the crowd on account of him.
And some of them were wishing to seize him, but no one laid hands upon him.
The officers therefore came to the chief priests and Pharisees, and those ones said to them, "Why did you not bring him?"
The officers answered, "Never did a man speak thus."
verses.note: GREEK: οὐδέποτε ἐλάλησεν οὕτως ἄνθρωπος (oudepote elalēsen houtōs anthrōpos)—"Never did a man speak thus." The officers sent to arrest Jesus return empty-handed, overcome by the authority of his speech. THEOLOGICAL: Even those sent by hostile authorities cannot help but bear witness to Jesus's unique authority, echoing the pattern of Mark 1:22 where crowds recognize teaching unlike the scribes.

verses.flow: The Pharisees therefore answered them, "Have you also been led astray?
Has any one of the rulers believed in him, or any of the Pharisees?

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But this crowd that does not know the law—they are accursed."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Pharisees' contempt for the crowd as "accursed" (ἐπάρατοί, eparatoi) reflects the attitude of some rabbinic authorities toward the ʿam ha-aretz ("people of the land"), the common people considered ignorant of Torah. This class disdain stands in sharp contrast to Jesus's invitation to "anyone who thirsts" (v.37).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Nicodemus said to them—the one who had come to him formerly, being one of them—
"Does our law judge the man unless it first hears from him and knows what he does?"
They answered and said to him, "Are you also from Galilee? Search and see that no prophet arises from Galilee."
verses.note: GREEK: ἐραύνησον (eraunēson, aorist imperative of ἐραυνάω) means "search" or "investigate thoroughly." HISTORICAL: The Pharisees' claim that no prophet arises from Galilee is historically inaccurate—Jonah was from Gath-hepher in Galilee (2 Kgs 14:25), and possibly Nahum and Hosea as well. Some manuscripts read "the prophet" (ὁ προφήτης) rather than "a prophet" (προφήτης), which would shift the claim to the specific eschatological Prophet of Deut 18:15. THEOLOGICAL: Their dismissal reveals prejudice overriding scriptural knowledge.
