verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Again therefore Jesus spoke to them, saying, "I am the light of the world; the one following me shall by no means walk in the darkness, but shall have the light of life."
verses.note: GREEK: "Egō eimi" (ἐγώ εἰμι) — the emphatic "I am" formula, echoing the divine self-disclosure of Exodus 3:14 (LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν). "Phōs" (φῶς, G5457) — "light" — a cosmic and revelatory term. Jesus claims the identity of the pillar of fire and the prophesied light of Isaiah 9:2 and 42:6. TYPOLOGY: The Feast of Tabernacles context (John 7:2) included the great lamp-lighting ceremony in the Court of Women, commemorating the pillar of fire. Jesus declares himself the fulfillment of that very symbol (Exod 13:21; Isa 60:19-20).

verses.flow: The Pharisees therefore said to him, "You bear witness concerning yourself; your witness is not true."
Jesus answered and said to them, "Even if I bear witness concerning myself, my witness is true, because I know whence I came and where I go; but you do not know whence I come or where I go."
"You judge according to the flesh; I judge no one."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "And yet even if I judge, my judgment is true, because I am not alone, but I and the Father who sent me."
"And even in your own law it has been written that the witness of two men is true."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "I am the one bearing witness concerning myself, and the Father who sent me bears witness concerning me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus identifies himself and the Father as the two legally required witnesses (Deut 19:15), thereby asserting a unique relational parity with the Father. This is not merely a legal argument but an ontological claim — the Father's witness is not external corroboration but unified divine testimony.

verses.flow: They were saying therefore to him, "Where is your Father?" Jesus answered, "You know neither me nor my Father. If you had known me, you would have known my Father also."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: These words he spoke in the treasury, teaching in the temple; and no one seized him, because his hour had not yet come.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "gazophylakion" (γαζοφυλάκιον, G1049) — the treasury — was located in the Court of Women within the temple complex, near the collection chests. This was a highly public teaching location. "His hour had not yet come" (cf. John 2:4; 7:30) reflects Johannine theology of divine sovereignty over the timing of the crucifixion.

verses.flow: He said therefore again to them, "I am going away, and you will seek me, and in your sin you will die. Where I go, you are not able to come."
The Jews therefore were saying, "Surely he will not kill himself, will he? Because he says, 'Where I go, you are not able to come.'"
And he was saying to them, "You are from below; I am from above. You are of this world; I am not of this world."
"I said therefore to you that you will die in your sins; for unless you believe that I am, you will die in your sins."
verses.note: GREEK: "Egō eimi" (ἐγώ εἰμι) here stands without a predicate — an absolute "I am" statement. This is the grammatical construction of divine self-identification from Exodus 3:14 (LXX) and Isaiah 43:10, 25; 45:18. The lack of predicate is theologically significant: Jesus claims the divine Name itself, not merely a title. THEOLOGICAL: Belief in Jesus' divine identity is presented as the condition for escaping death in sin — an extraordinary soteriological claim.

verses.flow: They were saying therefore to him, "Who are you?" Jesus said to them, "What I have been telling you from the beginning."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "I have many things to speak and to judge concerning you; but the one who sent me is true, and I — the things which I heard from him, these things I speak to the world."
They did not understand that he was speaking to them of the Father.
Jesus therefore said, "When you lift up the Son of Man, then you will know that I am, and that from myself I do nothing, but just as the Father taught me, these things I speak."
verses.note: GREEK: "Hypsōsēte" (ὑψώσητε, from ὑψόω, G5312) — "lift up" — carries a deliberate double meaning in John: physical lifting on the cross and exaltation to glory (cf. John 3:14; 12:32-34). TYPOLOGY: This echoes the lifting up of the bronze serpent in Numbers 21:8-9 (John 3:14). The crucifixion is simultaneously judgment, revelation, and exaltation. "Then you will know that I am" — the cross reveals Jesus' divine identity.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "And the one who sent me is with me. He has not left me alone, because I always do the things pleasing to him."

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: As he was speaking these things, many believed in him.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Jesus therefore was saying to the Jews who had believed him, "If you abide in my word, you are truly my disciples,"
"and you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Alētheia" (ἀλήθεια, G225) — "truth" — in Johannine theology is not merely propositional but personal (cf. John 14:6). The truth that liberates is the person of Jesus himself. "Eleutherōsei" (ἐλευθερώσει, G1659) — "will set free" — a future indicative, making freedom contingent upon knowing the truth through abiding in Christ's word (v. 31).

verses.flow: They answered him, "We are seed of Abraham, and have been enslaved to no one at any time. How do you say, 'You will become free'?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus answered them, "Truly, truly, I say to you, everyone who practices sin is a slave of sin."
"And the slave does not remain in the house unto the age; the Son remains unto the age."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Ho doulos" (the slave) versus "ho huios" (the Son) — a critical contrast. The slave has no permanent standing in the household; the Son has eternal standing. This anticipates the Pauline theology of adoption and inheritance (Gal 4:1-7; Rom 8:14-17). TYPOLOGY: Echoes the Hagar/Sarah typology — the son of the free woman inherits, the son of the slave does not (Gen 21:10; Gal 4:30).

verses.flow: "If therefore the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed."
"I know that you are seed of Abraham; but you seek to kill me, because my word has no place in you."
"What I have seen with the Father, I speak; and you therefore do what you have heard from your father."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus distinguishes two fathers and two patterns of imitation. He speaks what he has "seen" (heōraka, perfect tense — abiding vision) with his Father; they do what they have "heard" from their father. The contrast between divine origin and diabolic origin is sharpened in v. 44. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The language of "seeing" with the Father implies pre-existent communion in the divine council — Jesus has witnessed the heavenly reality firsthand (cf. John 1:18; 3:11-13).

verses.flow: They answered and said to him, "Our father is Abraham." Jesus said to them, "If you were children of Abraham, you would do the works of Abraham."
"But now you seek to kill me, a man who has spoken to you the truth which I heard from God. This Abraham did not do."
"You do the works of your father." They said to him, "We have not been born of fornication; we have one Father — God."
Jesus said to them, "If God were your Father, you would love me, for I came forth from God and have come; for neither have I come from myself, but that one sent me."
"Why do you not understand my speech? Because you are not able to hear my word."

"You are of your father the devil, and the desires of your father you will to do. That one was a murderer from the beginning, and has not stood in the truth, because truth is not in him. When he speaks the lie, he speaks from his own, because he is a liar and the father of it."
verses.note: GREEK: "Anthrōpoktonos" (ἀνθρωποκτόνος, G443) — "man-killer/murderer" — a compound word (anthrōpos + kteinō), used only here and in 1 John 3:15 in the NT. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The devil is identified as a rebellious spiritual being who was a murderer "from the beginning" (ap' archēs), alluding to the serpent's role in bringing death to humanity (Gen 3:1-19). He is characterized as the ontological source of falsehood — "the father of it." This places the adversary as the anti-type of God who is truth (John 14:6). TYPOLOGY: The murder "from the beginning" connects to the Cain narrative (Gen 4:8; 1 John 3:12), establishing a lineage of spiritual paternity through murderous deeds.

verses.flow: "But because I speak the truth, you do not believe me."
"Which of you convicts me of sin? If I speak truth, why do you not believe me?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "The one who is of God hears the words of God. On account of this you do not hear, because you are not of God."
The Jews answered and said to him, "Do we not say rightly that you are a Samaritan and have a demon?"
Jesus answered, "I do not have a demon, but I honor my Father, and you dishonor me."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "But I do not seek my own glory; there is one who seeks and judges."
"Truly, truly, I say to you, if anyone keeps my word, he shall by no means see death unto the age."
verses.note: GREEK: "Ou mē theōrēsē" (οὐ μὴ θεωρήσῃ) — the double negative with subjunctive is the strongest possible negation in Greek: "shall absolutely not see." "Thánatos" (θάνατος, G2288) — death — here signifies not merely physical death but eschatological death, eternal separation from God. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus claims authority over death itself, a prerogative belonging to God alone (Deut 32:39; 1 Sam 2:6). This provokes the charge of demon-possession in v. 52.

verses.flow: The Jews said to him, "Now we know that you have a demon. Abraham died, and the prophets, and you say, 'If anyone keeps my word, he shall by no means taste of death unto the age.'"
"Surely you are not greater than our father Abraham, who died? And the prophets died. Whom do you make yourself?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus answered, "If I glorify myself, my glory is nothing. It is my Father who glorifies me, of whom you say, 'He is our God.'"
"And you have not known him, but I know him. And if I should say that I do not know him, I will be like you — a liar. But I know him, and I keep his word."
"Abraham your father rejoiced that he might see my day, and he saw it and was glad."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Ēgalliasato" (ἠγαλλιάσατο, G21) — "rejoiced exceedingly" — Abraham's joy at seeing "my day" implies Abraham had prophetic revelation of the Messianic age. TYPOLOGY: This likely refers to Genesis 17:17 (Abraham's laughter at the promise of Isaac), Genesis 22 (the binding of Isaac as a type of Christ's sacrifice), or the broader Abrahamic covenant promises (Gen 12:1-3). Jewish tradition held that Abraham was given visions of future ages. Jesus claims that Abraham's entire covenantal hope found its fulfillment in him.

verses.flow: The Jews therefore said to him, "You are not yet fifty years old, and you have seen Abraham?"
Jesus said to them, "Truly, truly, I say to you, before Abraham came into being, I am."
verses.note: GREEK: "Prin Abraam genesthai, egō eimi" — "before Abraham came into being (genesthai, aorist infinitive of ginomai — to become/come into existence), I am (eimi, present tense)." The contrast between the aorist (point-in-time becoming) and the present (continuous being) is theologically decisive: Abraham came into existence; Jesus simply IS. THEOLOGICAL: This is the climactic "I am" statement of John 8, an unmistakable claim to the divine Name (Exod 3:14; Isa 41:4; 43:10, 13). The Jewish audience understood this perfectly, as their immediate attempt to stone him for blasphemy demonstrates (v. 59; cf. Lev 24:16).

verses.flow: They took up therefore stones that they might cast them at him; but Jesus hid himself and went out of the temple.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Stoning was the prescribed penalty for blasphemy under Torah (Lev 24:16). The crowd's response confirms they understood Jesus' "I am" as a claim to deity, not merely to pre-existence or prophetic authority. "Jesus hid himself" (ekrybē, aorist passive of kryptō) — the passive voice may suggest divine concealment; his hour had not yet come (cf. v. 20; Luke 4:30). He departed from the temple — the place of God's presence — even as he himself is the true Temple (John 2:19-21).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And passing by, he saw a man blind from birth.
verses.note: GREEK: "Passing by" (παράγων, parágōn) is a present participle of parágō, indicating continuous motion. "Blind from birth" (τυφλὸν ἐκ γενετῆς) — genetḗs (G1079) is used only here in the NT, a hapax legomenon meaning "from birth/origin," emphasizing the congenital and total nature of the blindness. THEOLOGICAL: The man's condition from birth sets the stage for a creative act, not merely a healing.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And his disciples asked him, saying, "Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he should be born blind?"
Jesus answered, "Neither did this man sin, nor his parents, but that the works of God might be made manifest in him."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus decisively rejects the retributive theology common in Second Temple Judaism that linked personal suffering directly to personal sin (cf. Job 4:7-8; Luke 13:1-5). The purpose clause "that the works of God might be made manifest" (ἵνα φανερωθῇ τὰ ἔργα τοῦ θεοῦ) reframes suffering within God's redemptive purpose rather than a punitive framework. TYPOLOGY: The manifestation of God's works through healing the blind echoes Isaiah 35:5 and the messianic expectation that the eyes of the blind would be opened.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: We must work the works of the one who sent me while it is day; night is coming, when no one is able to work.
When I am in the world, I am the light of the world.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' declaration "I am the light of the world" (φῶς εἰμι τοῦ κόσμου) echoes John 8:12 and constitutes one of the great "I am" (ἐγώ εἰμι) statements in John's Gospel. TYPOLOGY: This connects to Isaiah 42:6-7, where the Servant of the Lord is given as "a light to the nations, to open the eyes of the blind." The physical healing that follows dramatizes the spiritual reality of Christ as cosmic illuminator.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Having said these things, he spat on the ground and made clay from the spittle, and anointed his eyes with the clay,
verses.note: GREEK: "He spat" (ἔπτυσεν, éptysen) — ptýō (G4429). "Made clay" (ἐποίησεν πηλόν) — the verb poiéō echoes Genesis 2:7 LXX where God "formed" man. "Anointed" (ἐπέχρισεν, epéchrisen, G2025) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, a compound of epí + chríō ("to anoint upon"), linking the healing act to anointing. TYPOLOGY: Jesus forming clay and applying it to the man's eyes evokes God's original creation of Adam from the dust, suggesting a new creative act by the divine Word (John 1:3).

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and said to him, "Go, wash in the pool of Siloam" (which is interpreted, "Sent"). He went away therefore and washed, and came back seeing.
verses.note: GREEK: Siloam (Σιλωάμ) is interpreted as "Sent" (Ἀπεσταλμένος, Apestalménos), from apostéllō — the same root as "apostle." John's editorial note emphasizes that Jesus is the true "Sent One" from the Father (John 3:17, 5:36). HISTORICAL: The Pool of Siloam was located at the southern end of the City of David, fed by the Gihon Spring through Hezekiah's tunnel. It was a site of ritual purification, especially during Sukkot (cf. John 7:37-38). TYPOLOGY: The man must wash in the waters of the "Sent One" to receive sight — a picture of faith in Christ as the means of spiritual illumination.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The neighbors therefore, and those who had seen him formerly, that he was a beggar, were saying, "Is not this the one who was sitting and begging?"
Others were saying, "This is he." Others were saying, "No, but he is like him." That one was saying, "I am he."
They were saying therefore to him, "How then were your eyes opened?"

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: That one answered, "The man who is called Jesus made clay and anointed my eyes and said to me, 'Go to Siloam and wash.' Having gone away therefore and having washed, I received sight."
And they said to him, "Where is that one?" He says, "I do not know."
They bring him who was formerly blind to the Pharisees.
Now it was a Sabbath on the day when Jesus made the clay and opened his eyes.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Sabbath controversy is central to the Pharisaic opposition. Making clay (ἐποίησεν πηλόν) would violate the rabbinic prohibition against kneading on the Sabbath (m. Shabbat 7:2). Anointing the eyes could also be viewed as a prohibited act of healing on the Sabbath unless life was in danger. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' deliberate healing on the Sabbath asserts his lordship over the Sabbath (cf. Mark 2:28) and reveals that the Sabbath was meant for restorative divine work.

verses.flow: Again therefore the Pharisees also were asking him how he received sight. And he said to them, "He put clay upon my eyes, and I washed, and I see."

Therefore some of the Pharisees were saying, "This man is not from God, because he does not keep the Sabbath." Others were saying, "How is a sinful man able to do such signs?" And there was a division among them.
They say therefore to the blind man again, "What do you say concerning him, because he opened your eyes?" And he said, "He is a prophet."

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: The Jews therefore did not believe concerning him, that he had been blind and received sight, until they called the parents of the one who had received sight.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they asked them, saying, "Is this your son, whom you say was born blind? How then does he now see?"
His parents therefore answered and said, "We know that this is our son and that he was born blind.
But how he now sees, we do not know; or who opened his eyes, we do not know. Ask him; he is of age. He himself will speak concerning himself."
His parents said these things because they feared the Jews, for the Jews had already agreed that if anyone should confess him as Christ, he would be put out of the synagogue.
verses.note: GREEK: "Put out of the synagogue" (ἀποσυνάγωγος, aposynágōgos, G656) is a term unique to John's Gospel (also 12:42, 16:2), likely reflecting a formal ban or expulsion from the synagogue community. HISTORICAL: This may reflect the Birkat haMinim, a benediction against heretics added to the synagogue liturgy, which effectively excluded Jewish believers in Jesus. This term is significant evidence for the social cost of confessing Jesus as Christ in a Jewish context during and after Jesus' ministry.

verses.flow: Because of this his parents said, "He is of age; ask him."
They therefore called a second time the man who had been blind and said to him, "Give glory to God. We know that this man is a sinner."
verses.note: GREEK: "Give glory to God" (Δὸς δόξαν τῷ θεῷ) is a solemn adjuration formula meaning "tell the truth before God," an oath formula used in judicial settings (cf. Joshua 7:19 LXX). The Pharisees are not inviting praise but demanding the man recant his testimony. THEOLOGICAL: The irony is profound — they demand he glorify God while denying the very work of God performed before their eyes.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: That one therefore answered, "Whether he is a sinner, I do not know. One thing I do know: that though I was blind, now I see."
They said therefore to him, "What did he do to you? How did he open your eyes?"
He answered them, "I told you already, and you did not hear. Why do you wish to hear again? Surely you do not also wish to become his disciples?"
And they reviled him and said, "You are a disciple of that one, but we are disciples of Moses.
We know that God has spoken to Moses, but as for this one, we do not know from where he is."
The man answered and said to them, "For in this is the marvel, that you do not know from where he is, and yet he opened my eyes.
We know that God does not hear sinners, but if anyone is a worshipper of God and does his will, this one he hears.
verses.note: GREEK: "Worshipper of God" (θεοσεβής, theosebḗs, G2318) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, a compound of theós ("God") and sébomai ("to worship/revere"). It was used in Hellenistic Judaism for devout Gentile God-fearers and for the genuinely pious. THEOLOGICAL: The healed man articulates sound theology — God hears the godly, not the sinful — drawing on Psalm 34:15-16 and Proverbs 15:29. His theological reasoning exceeds that of the trained Pharisees.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: From the age it has never been heard that anyone opened the eyes of one born blind.
If this one were not from God, he would be able to do nothing."
They answered and said to him, "You were born entirely in sins, and you are teaching us?" And they cast him out.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus heard that they had cast him out, and having found him, he said, "Do you believe in the Son of Man?"
verses.note: GREEK: The textual variant here is significant: some manuscripts read "Son of God" (υἱὸν τοῦ θεοῦ) while the earliest and best manuscripts read "Son of Man" (υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου). THEOLOGICAL: "Son of Man" connects to Daniel 7:13-14 where the figure receives authority, glory, and an everlasting kingdom from the Ancient of Days. Jesus seeks out the one cast out by the religious establishment, revealing that exclusion from the synagogue leads to inclusion in the kingdom. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Son of Man title in Daniel's vision places Jesus within the divine council scene as the one who receives dominion from God himself.

verses.flow: That one answered and said, "And who is he, Lord, that I may believe in him?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus said to him, "You have both seen him, and the one speaking with you is that one."
And he said, "I believe, Lord." And he worshipped him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Worshipped" (προσεκύνησεν, prosekýnēsen, G4352) denotes prostration in reverent homage. In John's Gospel this term is reserved for worship directed toward God (John 4:20-24). THEOLOGICAL: That Jesus receives proskynesis without rebuke indicates his divine identity. Angels refuse such worship (Rev 19:10; 22:8-9), yet Jesus accepts it, consistent with John's presentation of him as the incarnate Word who is God (John 1:1, 1:18, 20:28).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus said, "For judgment I came into this world, that those who do not see may see, and that those who see may become blind."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus declares the purpose of his coming in terms of judgment (κρίμα, kríma) — not condemnatory judgment per se, but a division that reveals the true spiritual condition of all. Those who know they are blind receive sight; those who claim to see are exposed as blind. TYPOLOGY: This reversal echoes Isaiah 6:9-10 and the pattern of divine judgment throughout Scripture where God's revelation simultaneously saves and hardens (cf. 2 Cor 2:15-16). STRUCTURE: This verse forms the theological climax of the chapter, resolving the physical healing narrative into its spiritual meaning.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Those of the Pharisees who were with him heard these things, and said to him, "Are we also blind?"
Jesus said to them, "If you were blind, you would not have sin. But now you say, 'We see.' Your sin remains."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The final pronouncement seals the chapter's theme. The Pharisees' claim "We see" (Βλέπομεν) becomes the very ground of their condemnation. Self-declared spiritual competence in the presence of the Light of the World constitutes willful blindness, and therefore their sin "remains" (μένει, ménei — a characteristically Johannine verb of permanence). The chapter thus presents a complete inversion: the physically blind man progresses from blindness to sight to faith to worship, while the spiritually sighted Pharisees descend from claimed knowledge to willful rejection to abiding sin.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Truly, truly, I say to you, the one not entering through the door into the fold of the sheep, but climbing up from another way, that one is a thief and a robber.
verses.note: GREEK: "Aulē" (αὐλή, Strong's G833) refers to an enclosed courtyard or sheep-pen, not a barn. "Kléptēs" (κλέπτης, G2812) denotes a sneaky pilferer, while "lēistḗs" (λῃστής, G3027) denotes a violent brigand or bandit. The pairing covers both stealth and violence. THEOLOGICAL: This discourse follows directly from the healing of the man born blind (John 9) and constitutes Jesus's judgment on the Pharisees who cast the healed man out.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the one entering through the door is the shepherd of the sheep.
To this one the doorkeeper opens, and the sheep hear his voice, and his own sheep he calls by name and leads them out.
verses.note: GREEK: "Phōneî kat' onoma" (φωνεῖ κατ' ὄνομα)—"calls by name"—reflects the ancient Near Eastern shepherding practice where each sheep had an individual name. This implies intimate, personal knowledge. TYPOLOGY: Echoes Isaiah 43:1, "I have called you by name; you are mine," portraying Jesus as the divine Shepherd who knows each one personally (cf. John 10:14).

verses.flow: When he has put forth all his own, he goes before them, and the sheep follow him, because they know his voice.
But a stranger they will by no means follow, but will flee from him, because they do not know the voice of strangers.
This figure of speech Jesus spoke to them, but those ones did not understand what the things were which he was speaking to them.
Therefore Jesus said to them again, "Truly, truly, I say to you, I am the door of the sheep.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Egō eimi" (ἐγώ εἰμι) is the first of two "I am" declarations in this chapter (also v.11). This formula deliberately echoes the divine self-revelation in Exodus 3:14 (LXX: egō eimi ho ōn). Jesus identifies himself as the door (θύρα, thýra), the singular point of legitimate access to God's flock.

verses.flow: All who came before me are thieves and robbers, but the sheep did not hear them.
I am the door. If anyone enters through me, he shall be saved, and shall go in and go out, and shall find pasture.
verses.note: GREEK: "Nomē" (νομή, G3542) means "pasture" or "grazing ground"—a hapax legomenon in the Gospels (appears elsewhere only in 2 Tim 2:17 with a different sense). The triad "go in and go out and find pasture" echoes Numbers 27:17 and Ezekiel 34:14, where God promises to shepherd Israel himself. TYPOLOGY: The pattern of entering and going out freely recalls the covenant blessings of Deuteronomy 28:6.

verses.flow: The thief does not come except that he may steal and slaughter and destroy. I came that they may have life, and have it abundantly.
verses.note: GREEK: "Perisson" (περισσόν, G4053) means "exceedingly, abundantly, beyond measure." The contrast between the thief's purpose (steal, slaughter, destroy) and Jesus's purpose (life abundantly) is absolute. THEOLOGICAL: "Thýō" (θύω) means "to slaughter/sacrifice"—the thief performs a perverse sacrifice that destroys, whereas the Good Shepherd will offer the true sacrifice that gives life.

verses.flow: I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kalos" (καλός, G2570) means "good" in the sense of noble, beautiful, ideal—not merely "agathos" (morally good). "Tithēsin tēn psychēn" (τίθησιν τὴν ψυχήν) means literally "lays down the life/soul"—a voluntary, deliberate act. TYPOLOGY: Fulfills Ezekiel 34:23, "I will set up over them one shepherd, my servant David, and he shall feed them." Jesus is the promised Davidic Shepherd-King (cf. Ps 23:1; Zech 13:7).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: The hired servant, who is not a shepherd, whose own the sheep are not, sees the wolf coming and leaves the sheep and flees—and the wolf snatches them and scatters them—
because he is a hired servant and it does not matter to him concerning the sheep.
I am the good shepherd, and I know my own, and my own know me,
just as the Father knows me and I know the Father; and I lay down my life for the sheep.
And other sheep I have which are not of this fold; those also I must lead, and they shall hear my voice, and they shall become one flock, one shepherd.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Other sheep not of this fold" refers to Gentile believers who will be gathered into one flock with Jewish believers. This is a pivotal statement of the universal scope of Christ's saving work. TYPOLOGY: Fulfills Isaiah 56:8, "The Lord God, who gathers the outcasts of Israel, declares, 'I will gather yet others to him besides those already gathered.'" The "one flock, one shepherd" echoes Ezekiel 37:24.

verses.flow: For this reason the Father loves me, because I lay down my life, that I may take it again.
No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of myself. I have authority to lay it down, and I have authority to take it again. This commandment I received from my Father.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is among the most important christological statements in John. Jesus possesses "exousia" (ἐξουσία, G1849)—authority, rightful power—both to lay down his life and to take it up again. No mere human possesses authority over death. The "commandment" (entolē) from the Father indicates the crucifixion is not tragedy but divine commission, a voluntary covenantal act within the Godhead.

verses.flow: A division again arose among the Jews because of these words.
And many of them were saying, "He has a demon and is mad; why do you listen to him?"

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Others were saying, "These are not the sayings of one who is demonized. Is a demon able to open the eyes of the blind?"
Then the Feast of Dedication took place in Jerusalem. It was winter,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Ta enkainia" (τὰ ἐγκαίνια, G1456) is the Feast of Dedication, known today as Hanukkah, commemorating the rededication of the Temple by Judas Maccabeus in 164 BC after its desecration by Antiochus IV Epiphanes (1 Macc 4:36-59). The winter setting (cheimōn) places this around December. The feast's theme of temple consecration provides the backdrop for Jesus's claim to be the one "whom the Father sanctified" (v.36).

verses.flow: and Jesus was walking in the temple, in the Porch of Solomon.
The Jews therefore surrounded him and were saying to him, "How long do you hold our souls in suspense? If you are the Christ, tell us plainly."
Jesus answered them, "I told you, and you do not believe. The works which I do in the name of my Father, these bear witness concerning me.
But you do not believe, because you are not of my sheep.
My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me.
And I give to them eternal life, and they shall by no means perish unto the age, and no one shall snatch them out of my hand.
verses.note: GREEK: The double negative "ou mē" (οὐ μή) with the aorist subjunctive "apolōntai" (ἀπόλωνται) expresses the strongest possible negation in Greek: "they shall absolutely never perish." "Eis ton aiōna" (εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα) means "unto the age," i.e., forever. THEOLOGICAL: Combined with v.29, this constitutes the doctrine of the double security of the believer—held in both the Son's hand and the Father's hand.

verses.flow: My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all, and no one is able to snatch them out of the hand of the Father.
I and the Father are one."
verses.note: GREEK: "Hen" (ἕν) is neuter, not masculine (heis). Jesus does not say "we are one person" but "we are one thing"—one in essence, nature, and will. The plural verb "esmen" (ἐσμεν) preserves the distinction of persons. THEOLOGICAL: This is a foundational text for Trinitarian theology—unity of divine essence with distinction of persons. The Jews understood this as a claim to deity (v.33), confirming that Jesus intended a claim to divine nature, not merely moral agreement.

verses.flow: Therefore the Jews again took up stones that they might stone him.
Jesus answered them, "Many good works I showed you from the Father; on account of which of them do you stone me?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The Jews answered him, "Concerning a good work we do not stone you, but concerning blasphemy, and because you, being a man, make yourself God."

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: Jesus answered them, "Is it not written in your Law, 'I said, you are gods'?
verses.note: GREEK: Jesus quotes Psalm 82:6 (LXX 81:6): "Egō eipa, theoi este" (Ἐγὼ εἶπα, Θεοί ἐστε). DIVINE_COUNCIL: Psalm 82 depicts God (Elohim) standing in the divine council (adat-El) and judging the "gods" (elohim)—divine beings or spiritual rulers who failed in their commission to execute justice. Jesus's argument is a fortiori: if Scripture itself applies "theoi" to those lesser beings who received God's word, how much more does the title belong to the one whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world.

verses.flow: If he called them gods, to whom the word of God came—and the Scripture cannot be broken—
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Scripture cannot be broken" (ou dynatai lythēnai hē graphē) is one of the strongest affirmations of scriptural authority from Jesus himself. "Lythēnai" (λυθῆναι, from lyō) means "to loose, dissolve, destroy." DIVINE_COUNCIL: Jesus affirms the reality of the Psalm 82 divine council scene as authoritative Scripture—beings were genuinely called "theoi" by God's own decree, establishing the legitimacy of the term applied to commissioned divine agents.

verses.flow: do you say of him whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world, 'You blaspheme,' because I said, 'I am the Son of God'?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Whom the Father sanctified (hēgiasen) and sent (apesteilen) into the world"—the double action of consecration and sending establishes Jesus's unique status. He is not merely a recipient of God's word like the Psalm 82 figures; he is the one set apart from eternity and dispatched on divine mission. His claim to be "Son of God" (Huios tou Theou) is thus categorically higher than the "theoi" designation of Psalm 82.

verses.flow: If I do not do the works of my Father, do not believe me.
But if I do them, even if you do not believe me, believe the works, that you may know and continue to know that the Father is in me, and I in the Father."
verses.note: GREEK: The textual variant between "ginōskēte" (present subjunctive—"continue to know") and "gnōte" (aorist subjunctive—"come to know") suggests progressive deepening of knowledge. THEOLOGICAL: "The Father is in me, and I in the Father" (en emoi ho patēr kagō en tō patri) expresses mutual indwelling or perichoresis—a concept central to later Trinitarian formulation. This is stronger than v.30, expressing interpenetration of persons within the divine unity.

verses.flow: Therefore they were seeking again to seize him, and he went forth out of their hand.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he went away again beyond the Jordan, to the place where John was first baptizing, and he remained there.
And many came to him and were saying, "John indeed performed no sign, but all things whatsoever John said concerning this one were true."
And many believed in him there.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now a certain man was sick, Lazarus from Bethany, from the village of Mary and Martha her sister.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And it was Mariam who had anointed the Lord with ointment and wiped his feet with her hair, whose brother Lazarus was sick.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: Therefore the sisters sent to him, saying, "Lord, behold, he whom you love is sick."
verses.note: GREEK: The sisters use philéō (φιλεῖς, Strong's G5368), denoting affectionate friendship-love, rather than agapáō. This intimate term emphasizes the personal bond between Jesus and Lazarus, making the deliberate delay in v.6 all the more striking.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But when Jesus heard it, he said, "This sickness is not unto death, but for the glory of God, that the Son of God may be glorified through it."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus declares the sickness is "not unto death" (πρὸς θάνατον) — not meaning Lazarus would not die physically, but that death was not its ultimate purpose. The true purpose is the glorification of the Son of God, linking this sign to the broader Johannine theme of Jesus' glory revealed through signs (John 2:11). The phrase "the Son of God" here anticipates Martha's confession in v.27.

verses.flow: Now Jesus loved Martha and her sister and Lazarus.
verses.note: GREEK: John switches to agapáō (ἠγάπα, Strong's G25) here, the verb of deliberate, covenantal love, distinct from the philéō used by the sisters in v.3. This deliberate juxtaposition may indicate that Jesus' love was of a higher, purposeful order — a love that could embrace even painful delay for a greater good.

verses.flow: When therefore he heard that he was sick, then indeed he remained in the place where he was for two days.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The two-day delay is deeply intentional. By the time Jesus arrives (v.17), Lazarus has been dead four days. Jewish tradition held that the soul lingered near the body for three days; by day four, decomposition confirmed death was irreversible. Jesus' delay ensures the miracle is beyond all dispute.

verses.flow: Then after this he says to the disciples, "Let us go into Judea again."
The disciples say to him, "Rabbi, the Jews were just now seeking to stone you, and you are going there again?"
Jesus answered, "Are there not twelve hours in the day? If anyone walks in the day, he does not stumble, because he sees the light of this world.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus employs a metaphor of day and night that echoes John 8:12 and 9:4-5, where he identifies himself as the light of the world. Walking "in the day" means operating within God's appointed time and purpose. The twelve hours signify a divinely ordained period of ministry that cannot be shortened by human threat.

verses.flow: But if anyone walks in the night, he stumbles, because the light is not in him."
These things he said, and after this he says to them, "Lazarus our friend has fallen asleep, but I am going that I may awaken him."
Therefore the disciples said to him, "Lord, if he has fallen asleep, he will be saved."
Now Jesus had spoken concerning his death, but they supposed that he was speaking concerning the rest of sleep.
verses.note: GREEK: The word koímēsis (κοίμησις, Strong's G2838) is a NT hapax legomenon, appearing only here. It means "a falling asleep" or "rest in sleep." The common verb koimáomai was widely used as a euphemism for death among early Christians (1 Cor 15:6, 1 Thess 4:13), but the disciples take it literally.

verses.flow: Then therefore Jesus said to them plainly, "Lazarus has died.
And I rejoice on account of you, that you may believe, because I was not there. But let us go to him."

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore Thomas, the one called Didymus, said to his fellow disciples, "Let us also go, that we may die with him."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Thomas (Θωμᾶς) is the Aramaic name meaning "twin," which John translates with the Greek Dídymos (Δίδυμος). His statement "let us die with him" reveals both loyalty and a pessimistic expectation, foreshadowing his later doubt (John 20:25). Yet it also ironically anticipates the call to discipleship as dying with Christ (Rom 6:8).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Then when Jesus came, he found him already having been four days in the tomb.
Now Bethany was near Jerusalem, about fifteen stadia away.
And many of the Jews had come to Martha and Mariam, that they might comfort them concerning their brother.
Therefore Martha, when she heard that Jesus was coming, went to meet him; but Mary was sitting in the house.
Therefore Martha said to Jesus, "Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died.
But even now I know that whatever you ask of God, God will give to you."
Jesus says to her, "Your brother will rise again."
Martha says to him, "I know that he will rise again in the resurrection at the last day."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus said to her, "I am the resurrection and the life. The one believing in me, even if he should die, will live.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is the fifth of Jesus' seven "I am" (ἐγώ εἰμι) declarations in John, and arguably the most theologically dense. Jesus does not merely promise resurrection — he declares himself to be resurrection and life in his very person. The one who believes in him, even if he dies physically, shall live. This collapses the eschatological hope of "the last day" (v.24) into the present person of Jesus (realized eschatology). TYPOLOGY: This fulfills and transcends the hope expressed in Daniel 12:2 and Ezekiel 37:1-14, where God alone raises the dead. Jesus claims that divine prerogative as his own identity.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And everyone who lives and believes in me shall never die unto the age. Do you believe this?"
verses.note: GREEK: The double negative οὐ μὴ (ou mē) with the aorist subjunctive ἀποθάνῃ creates the strongest possible negation in Greek: "shall absolutely never die unto the age (εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα)." This promises not merely survival but eternal, unending life for the one who lives and believes in Jesus.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: She says to him, "Yes, Lord; I have believed that you are the Christ, the Son of God, the one coming into the world."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Martha's confession is among the most complete christological statements in the Gospels, paralleling Peter's confession (Matt 16:16). She affirms three titles: "the Christ" (ὁ Χριστός — the Anointed One, Israel's promised King), "the Son of God" (ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ — divine sonship), and "the one coming into the world" (ὁ εἰς τὸν κόσμον ἐρχόμενος — the expected eschatological figure of Mal 3:1 and Deut 18:15).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And having said this, she went away and called Mariam her sister secretly, saying, "The Teacher is here and is calling for you."
And when she heard this, she rose quickly and was coming to him.
Now Jesus had not yet come into the village, but was still in the place where Martha had met him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore the Jews who were with her in the house and were comforting her, seeing Mariam that she rose quickly and went out, followed her, supposing that she was going to the tomb to weep there.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Therefore Mariam, when she came to where Jesus was, seeing him, fell at his feet, saying to him, "Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died."

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular feminine
verb aorist active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore when Jesus saw her weeping, and the Jews who had come with her weeping, he was deeply moved in spirit and troubled himself,
verses.note: GREEK: The verb ἐνεβριμήσατο (enebrimḗsato, Strong's G1690) is extraordinarily strong — it denotes a deep snorting or groaning, often associated with indignation or intense agitation, not mere sorrow. Combined with ἐτάραξεν ἑαυτόν ("he troubled himself"), this reveals Jesus' profound emotional response. Some scholars see anger at death itself as an enemy, others at the unbelief surrounding him, or at the cosmic power of death over God's creation. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Jesus as the incarnate Son confronts the domain of death — the realm of the enemy — with visceral opposition, anticipating his own victory over death.

verses.flow: and said, "Where have you laid him?" They say to him, "Lord, come and see."
Jesus wept.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐδάκρυσεν (edákrysen, Strong's G1145) is an aorist indicative, indicating a single, definitive act of weeping. This is distinct from the ongoing weeping (klaíō, present tense) of Mary and the Jews in v.33. The shortest verse in Scripture reveals the full humanity of the incarnate Word (John 1:14) — the one who is the resurrection and the life nevertheless weeps at the grave of his friend.

verses.flow: Therefore the Jews were saying, "Behold how he loved him!"

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But some of them said, "Was not this one, who opened the eyes of the blind man, able to do something that this man also should not have died?"

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore Jesus, again being deeply moved within himself, comes to the tomb. Now it was a cave, and a stone was lying upon it.
verses.note: GREEK: John again uses ἐμβριμώμενος (embrimṓmenos), the same strong verb from v.33, indicating Jesus' continued deep agitation as he approaches the tomb. HISTORICAL: Rock-cut tomb caves sealed with stones were common in first-century Judean burial practice. The stone could be a circular rolling stone or a flat slab placed over the entrance.

words.m: verb perfect active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus says, "Take away the stone." Martha, the sister of the one who had died, says to him, "Lord, already he stinks, for it is the fourth day."
verses.note: GREEK: The word τεταρταῖος (tetartaîos, Strong's G5066) means "of the fourth day" and appears only here in the NT (hapax legomenon). Martha's protest about the stench underscores the finality of death and the magnitude of the miracle about to occur. HISTORICAL: The detail of four days confirms death beyond any possibility of resuscitation by ancient medical understanding, countering any claim that Lazarus was merely in a coma.

verses.flow: Jesus says to her, "Did I not say to you that if you believe, you will see the glory of God?"
Therefore they took away the stone. And Jesus lifted his eyes upward and said, "Father, I thank you that you heard me.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And I knew that you always hear me, but on account of the crowd standing around I said it, that they may believe that you sent me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' prayer reveals his perfect, unbroken communion with the Father. He states he knew the Father always hears him — the prayer is spoken aloud for the sake of the crowd, that they might believe the Father sent him. This models the Johannine theme of the sent Son (John 3:17; 5:36; 17:3) whose every act reveals the Father's will and authority.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having said these things, he cried out with a loud voice, "Lazarus, come forth!"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus commands with a loud voice (φωνῇ μεγάλῃ) — the same phrase used of the eschatological shout in 1 Thessalonians 4:16. He calls Lazarus by name, exercising sovereign authority over death. TYPOLOGY: This anticipates John 5:28-29, where all who are in the tombs will hear the voice of the Son of God and come forth. The raising of Lazarus is the enacted sign of the final resurrection.

verses.flow: The one who had died came forth, bound at the feet and the hands with burial wrappings, and his face had been wrapped around with a cloth. Jesus says to them, "Loose him and let him go."
verses.note: GREEK: The term κειρίαις (keiríais, Strong's G2750) refers to linen strips or burial wrappings, distinct from the σουδαρίῳ (soudáriō, Strong's G4676), a Latin loanword (sudarium) referring to a face cloth or sweat cloth. TYPOLOGY: The grave clothes from which Lazarus must be freed contrast with Jesus' own resurrection in John 20:6-7, where the burial cloths are left behind in orderly fashion — Jesus needed no one to loose him.

verses.flow: Therefore many of the Jews who had come to Mariam and had beheld what he did, believed in him.
But some of them went away to the Pharisees and told them the things which Jesus had done.
Therefore the chief priests and the Pharisees gathered a council and were saying, "What are we doing? For this man is performing many signs.
If we leave him thus, all will believe in him, and the Romans will come and will take away both our place and our nation."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Our place" (τὸν τόπον) likely refers to the Temple, and "our nation" (τὸ ἔθνος) to the Jewish people under their governance. The Sanhedrin fears Roman military intervention if a messianic movement grows. This is deeply ironic: their plot to kill Jesus to preserve the Temple will ultimately lead to its destruction in AD 70.

verses.flow: But a certain one of them, Caiaphas, being high priest of that year, said to them, "You know nothing,
nor do you consider that it is expedient for you that one man should die on behalf of the people, and that the whole nation should not perish."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Caiaphas argues cold political expediency — better one man die than the nation perish. Yet John immediately identifies this as unwitting prophecy (v.51). The high priest, by virtue of his office, speaks a truth beyond his own understanding: Jesus will indeed die "on behalf of" (ὑπέρ) the people — the language of substitutionary atonement (Isa 53:5-6).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now this he did not say from himself, but being high priest of that year, he prophesied that Jesus was about to die on behalf of the nation,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: John's editorial comment transforms political calculation into divine prophecy. The high priestly office, despite Caiaphas' corrupt intentions, becomes a vehicle of divine revelation. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This reflects the biblical pattern where God sovereignly uses even adversarial agents to accomplish his redemptive purposes (cf. Gen 50:20; Acts 2:23).

verses.flow: and not on behalf of the nation only, but that he might also gather into one the children of God who had been scattered abroad.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "scattered children of God" (τὰ τέκνα τοῦ θεοῦ τὰ διεσκορπισμένα) echoes the prophetic hope of regathering dispersed Israel (Isa 11:12; Ezek 34:12-13) and extends it to include Gentile believers — the "other sheep" of John 10:16. The gathering "into one" (εἰς ἕν) anticipates the unity prayer of John 17:20-23 and the Pauline vision of one body from Jew and Gentile (Eph 2:14-16).

verses.flow: Therefore from that day they took counsel together that they might put him to death.

verses.flow: Therefore Jesus no longer walked openly among the Jews, but went away from there into the region near the wilderness, to a city called Ephraim, and there he remained with the disciples.
Now the Passover of the Jews was near, and many went up to Jerusalem from the region before the Passover, that they might purify themselves.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Passover (τὸ πάσχα) was one of three pilgrimage festivals requiring Jews to travel to Jerusalem. Ritual purification before the feast was required for those who had contracted ceremonial uncleanness (Num 9:10-13; 2 Chr 30:17-18). Many pilgrims arrived early to undergo purification rites. TYPOLOGY: The proximity of Passover frames the entire Lazarus narrative within the theology of the paschal lamb — Jesus, who just raised the dead, now moves toward his own sacrificial death as the true Passover Lamb (John 1:29).

verses.flow: Therefore they were seeking Jesus and were saying to one another, standing in the temple, "What do you think? That he will surely not come to the feast?"
Now the chief priests and the Pharisees had given orders that if anyone should know where he is, he should report it, so that they might seize him.
Therefore Jesus, six days before the Passover, came to Bethany, where Lazarus was, whom Jesus had raised from the dead.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Bethany was a village about two miles east of Jerusalem on the southeastern slope of the Mount of Olives. "Six days before the Passover" places this event on the Saturday before Passion Week, establishing the precise chronological framework John provides for Jesus's final days.

verses.flow: Therefore they made him a supper there, and Martha was serving, and Lazarus was one of those reclining at table with him.

verses.flow: Then Mary, having taken a pound of ointment of pure nard, very costly, anointed the feet of Jesus and wiped his feet with her hair; and the house was filled with the fragrance of the ointment.
verses.note: GREEK: "Nard" (nárdos, G3487) refers to spikenard, an aromatic plant from the Himalayas; "pistikēs" (G4101) is debated—likely meaning "genuine" or "pure," possibly from pistis (faithfulness/trustworthiness). The "litra" (G3046) is a Roman pound, approximately twelve ounces. TYPOLOGY: Mary's anointing of Jesus's feet anticipates his burial preparation (v.7), inverting the expected anointing of a king's head—she worships the King who will reign through death.

verses.flow: But Judas the Iscariot, one of his disciples, the one being about to betray him, says,
"Why was this ointment not sold for three hundred denarii and given to the poor?"

verses.flow: But he said this not because he was concerned about the poor, but because he was a thief, and having the money-box, he was carrying away what was put into it.
verses.note: GREEK: "Glōssokomon" (G1101) originally meant a case for holding the mouthpieces (glossai) of wind instruments, then came to mean any small box or chest. It appears only here and in John 13:29 in the NT. "Ebstazen" (bastazō, G941) carries the double meaning of "to carry" and "to pilfer/carry away," and John exploits this ambiguity deliberately.

verses.flow: Therefore Jesus said, "Leave her alone, that she may keep it for the day of my burial.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus reinterprets Mary's act as prophetic preparation for his burial (entaphiasmos, G1780). This is one of the clearest moments where Jesus openly acknowledges his approaching death and frames a spontaneous act of devotion within the divine plan of his sacrificial death.

verses.flow: For the poor you always have with you, but me you do not always have."
Therefore a great crowd of the Jews learned that he was there, and they came not on account of Jesus only, but that they might also see Lazarus, whom he had raised from the dead.
But the chief priests resolved that they might put Lazarus also to death,
because on account of him many of the Jews were going away and believing in Jesus.

verses.flow: took the branches of palm trees and went out to meet him, and were crying out, "Hosanna! Blessed is the one coming in the name of the Lord, even the King of Israel!"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The crowd's acclamation quotes Psalm 118:25-26 (LXX 117), a Hallel psalm sung at Passover and Tabernacles. "Hosanna" (hōsanna, G5614) is a transliteration of Hebrew hoshi'a-na, "Save now!" The addition of "the King of Israel" explicitly identifies Jesus as the Davidic Messiah entering his capital (cf. Zeph 3:15; Zech 9:9). STRUCTURE: Palm branches were a symbol of Jewish nationalism and victory, recalling the Maccabean triumph (1 Macc 13:51).

verses.flow: And Jesus, having found a young donkey, sat upon it, just as it is written,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "Fear not, daughter of Zion; behold, your King comes, sitting upon the colt of a donkey."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This quotation conflates Zephaniah 3:16 ("Fear not, daughter of Zion") with Zechariah 9:9 ("your king comes...sitting upon a colt"). Zechariah's context presents a humble king who brings peace to the nations—the opposite of a military conqueror. John's selective quotation omits "righteous and having salvation, humble" to let the image of the donkey speak for itself.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: These things his disciples did not understand at first, but when Jesus was glorified, then they remembered that these things had been written concerning him, and that they had done these things to him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: John's editorial comment reveals a hermeneutical principle: the disciples understood Scripture's Christological meaning only after Jesus's glorification (his death, resurrection, and ascension) and the coming of the Spirit (cf. John 14:26; 16:13). This retrospective illumination is a major Johannine theme.

verses.flow: Therefore the crowd that was with him when he called Lazarus out of the tomb and raised him from the dead was bearing witness.
For this reason also the crowd went to meet him, because they heard that he had done this sign.
Therefore the Pharisees said to one another, "You see that you are gaining nothing. Behold, the world has gone after him."

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Now there were certain Greeks among those going up to worship at the feast.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Hellēnes" (G1672) here likely refers to Greek-speaking Gentile God-fearers or proselytes who came to worship at the Jerusalem feast, not ethnic Jews from the Diaspora (who would be called "Hellenists"). Their approach to Jesus through Philip—who bore a Greek name and came from the bilingual region of Bethsaida—signals the universal scope of Jesus's mission. THEOLOGICAL: The arrival of Gentiles seeking Jesus triggers his declaration that "the hour has come" (v.23), linking Gentile inclusion to the cross.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: These therefore came to Philip, the one from Bethsaida of Galilee, and were asking him, saying, "Lord, we wish to see Jesus."
Philip comes and tells Andrew; Andrew and Philip come and tell Jesus.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus answers them, saying, "The hour has come that the Son of Man should be glorified.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The hour" (hē hōra) is a central Johannine concept—previously "not yet come" (2:4; 7:30; 8:20), it now arrives. The glorification (doxazō, G1392) of the Son of Man in John paradoxically includes both crucifixion and exaltation as a single event. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title "Son of Man" (ho huios tou anthrōpou) echoes Daniel 7:13-14, where a divine-human figure receives universal dominion from the Ancient of Days—a throne-room scene within the divine council.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Truly, truly, I say to you, unless the grain of wheat, having fallen into the earth, dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kokkos tou sitou" (G2848, G4621)—literally "grain of the wheat." The agricultural metaphor of death producing life is unique to John among the Gospels. TYPOLOGY: This seed parable encapsulates the entire paschal mystery: Christ's death is not defeat but the necessary condition for the eschatological harvest of redeemed humanity (cf. 1 Cor 15:36-37).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one loving his life loses it, and the one hating his life in this world shall keep it unto life eternal.
If anyone serves me, let him follow me; and where I am, there also my servant shall be. If anyone serves me, the Father will honor him.
Now my soul is troubled, and what shall I say? Father, save me from this hour? But for this purpose I came to this hour.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus's soul-trouble (tetaraktai, perfect passive of tarassō, G5015) parallels the Gethsemane agony in the Synoptics (Matt 26:38; Mark 14:34) but is presented in distinctly Johannine fashion. The rhetorical question "Father, save me from this hour?" echoes Psalm 6:3-4 (LXX) and Psalm 42:5-6. Jesus answers his own question—he will not pray to escape the hour, for he came precisely for this purpose.

verses.flow: Father, glorify your name." Therefore a voice came out of heaven, "I have both glorified it, and I will glorify it again."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The heavenly voice (bat qol in rabbinic tradition) represents a direct theophanic intervention—the Father speaking audibly from the heavenly realm into the earthly. This is the third heavenly declaration about Jesus in the Gospels (cf. baptism, Matt 3:17; transfiguration, Matt 17:5). "I have glorified and will glorify again" spans the entire scope of Jesus's ministry and points forward to the cross and resurrection.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore the crowd that stood by and heard it was saying that thunder had occurred. Others were saying, "An angel has spoken to him."
Jesus answered and said, "This voice has not come for my sake, but for yours.
Now is the judgment of this world; now the ruler of this world shall be cast out.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The ruler of this world" (ho archōn tou kosmou toutou) identifies Satan as the usurping authority over the present age. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This verse reflects divine council theology—Satan held a position of delegated authority over the nations (cf. Dan 10:13, 20; Luke 4:6; Eph 2:2), and the cross constitutes his formal expulsion (ekblēthēsetai, G1544, future passive) from that position of power. "Now is the judgment" declares the eschatological turning point accomplished through Christ's death.

verses.flow: And I, if I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all to myself."
verses.note: GREEK: "Hypsōthō" (G5312, from hypsoō, "to lift up/exalt") carries a deliberate double meaning in John: physical lifting up on the cross and theological exaltation to glory (cf. John 3:14; 8:28). "Helkysō" (G1670, "I will draw") suggests a powerful, irresistible attraction. "Pantas" (all) is masculine accusative—"all people," signaling universal scope transcending Jewish boundaries.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But this he was saying, signifying by what kind of death he was about to die.
Therefore the crowd answered him, "We have heard from the Law that the Christ remains forever, and how do you say that the Son of Man must be lifted up? Who is this Son of Man?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The crowd's confusion reveals a tension in Second Temple messianic expectation. "The Law" (ho nomos) here broadly refers to Scripture, citing traditions from Psalm 89:36, Isaiah 9:7, Ezekiel 37:25, and Daniel 7:14 about the Messiah's eternal reign. They cannot reconcile an eternal Messiah with one who must be "lifted up" (die). This misunderstanding exposes their inability to grasp the paradox of glorification through crucifixion.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore Jesus said to them, "Yet a little while the light is among you. Walk while you have the light, lest the darkness overtake you; and the one walking in the darkness does not know where he goes.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: While you have the light, believe in the light, that you may become sons of light." These things Jesus spoke, and departing, he hid himself from them.

words.m: verb perfect active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: But though he had done so many signs before them, they were not believing in him,
that the word of Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled, which he spoke: "Lord, who has believed our report? And to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed?"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: John quotes Isaiah 53:1 (LXX), the opening of the fourth Servant Song. This is a critical text linking Jesus's rejection to the suffering Servant of Isaiah. "The arm of the Lord" (ho brachiōn kyriou) is a divine power metaphor for God's saving acts, now revealed paradoxically in the weakness of the cross.

verses.flow: For this reason they were not able to believe, because again Isaiah said,
"He has blinded their eyes and hardened their heart, lest they should see with their eyes and understand with their heart and turn, and I should heal them."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This quotation from Isaiah 6:10 (LXX, slightly adapted) raises the profound question of divine sovereignty and human responsibility in unbelief. The hardening language echoes the Exodus pattern (Ex 4:21; 7:3). John presents Israel's rejection not as a failure of God's plan but as its fulfillment—the same prophetic word that commissioned Isaiah now explains the rejection of the one Isaiah foresaw.

verses.flow: These things Isaiah said because he saw his glory, and he spoke concerning him.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: John states that Isaiah "saw his glory" (eiden tēn doxan autou), referring to the theophanic vision of Isaiah 6:1-4, where Isaiah beheld the Lord (YHWH) seated on his throne surrounded by the seraphim. John identifies this glory as the pre-incarnate glory of Christ, making an extraordinary Christological claim: the one whom Isaiah saw enthroned in the heavenly council was the Son himself (cf. John 1:1, 18; 17:5).

verses.flow: Nevertheless, even among the rulers many believed in him, but on account of the Pharisees they were not confessing, lest they should become expelled from the synagogue;
verses.note: GREEK: "Aposynagōgoi" (G656) is a term found only in John (9:22; 12:42; 16:2), referring to formal expulsion from synagogue fellowship. HISTORICAL: This reflects the growing tension between the Johannine community and the synagogue, possibly connected to the Birkat ha-Minim (benediction against heretics) introduced in the late first century.

verses.flow: for they loved the glory of men more than the glory of God.

verses.flow: But Jesus cried out and said, "The one believing in me does not believe in me, but in the one who sent me.

verses.flow: And the one beholding me beholds the one who sent me.

verses.flow: I have come as light into the world, that everyone believing in me should not remain in the darkness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus's self-identification as "light" (phōs, G5457) echoes the prologue (John 1:4-9) and the Feast of Tabernacles declaration (John 8:12). "I have come into the world" (eis ton kosmon elēlytha) asserts pre-existence and divine mission. The purpose clause—"that everyone believing in me should not remain in darkness"—frames salvation as illumination, transferring believers from the domain of darkness into the realm of divine light (cf. Col 1:13; 1 John 1:5-7).

verses.flow: And if anyone hears my words and does not keep them, I do not judge him; for I did not come that I might judge the world, but that I might save the world.

verses.flow: The one rejecting me and not receiving my words has one who judges him: the word that I spoke, that will judge him on the last day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus distinguishes between present and future judgment. He does not judge now (in his first coming), yet his spoken word (ho logos) becomes the eschatological judge on "the last day" (tē eschatē hēmera, G2078 + G2250). This personalizes the word as a living, active agent of judgment (cf. Heb 4:12), reflecting the OT concept of the divine word that accomplishes its purpose (Isa 55:11).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Because I have not spoken from myself, but the Father who sent me, he himself has given me a commandment, what I should say and what I should speak.
And I know that his commandment is life eternal. Therefore the things that I speak, just as the Father has spoken to me, so I speak."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter concludes with Jesus's affirmation that the Father's commandment "is life eternal" (zōē aiōnios, G2222 + G166)—eternal life is not merely a future reward but is embedded in the very content of the Father's word spoken through the Son. Jesus's complete obedience to the Father's command regarding what to say and speak establishes him as the definitive and final prophet greater than Moses (cf. Deut 18:18-19; John 1:17).

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now before the feast of the Passover, Jesus, knowing that His hour had come that He should depart out of this world unto the Father, having loved His own who were in the world, He loved them unto the end.
verses.note: GREEK: "Eis telos" (εἰς τέλος) can mean both "to the end" (i.e., to the uttermost completion) and "to the uttermost degree." The word telos (G5056) carries both temporal and qualitative force. THEOLOGICAL: This verse frames the entire Passion narrative as the supreme act of Christ's love — not compelled but deliberate, proceeding from full knowledge of His divine mission. TYPOLOGY: The Passover setting links Jesus' act of love to the Exodus deliverance; He is both the Paschal Lamb and the One who serves (Luke 22:27).

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive singular neuter
verb perfect active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And supper having come, the devil having already cast it into the heart that Judas, son of Simon Iscariot, should betray Him,

verses.flow: knowing that the Father had given all things into His hands, and that He came forth from God and was going back unto God,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes a remarkable Christological claim: Jesus acts from full consciousness of His divine origin ("He came forth from God"), His divine authority ("all things into His hands"), and His divine destination ("going back unto God"). Yet from this summit of authority He descends to the role of the lowest household servant. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Father's giving of "all things" (panta) into the Son's hands echoes the universal dominion granted in Daniel 7:14 and Psalm 8:6.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: He rises from the supper and lays aside His garments, and having taken a towel, He girded Himself.

verses.flow: Then He pours water into the basin and began to wash the feet of the disciples and to wipe them with the towel with which He was girded.
He comes therefore to Simon Peter. He says to him, "Lord, do You wash my feet?"
Jesus answered and said to him, "What I am doing you do not understand now, but you shall know after these things."
Peter says to Him, "You shall never wash my feet!" Jesus answered him, "If I do not wash you, you have no part with Me."
verses.note: GREEK: "Ou mē" (οὐ μή) with the aorist subjunctive is the strongest form of negation in Greek — Peter's refusal is emphatic and absolute: "by no means, never." THEOLOGICAL: "Meros" (μέρος, G3313) — "part" or "portion" — carries covenantal weight. To have no part with Jesus is to be excluded from His inheritance and kingdom. Christ's washing is not optional humility but a necessary act of cleansing grace.

verses.flow: Simon Peter says to Him, "Lord, not my feet only, but also the hands and the head!"

words.m: verb perfect middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus says to him, "The one who has been bathed has no need except to wash the feet, but is wholly clean. And you are clean, but not all."
verses.note: GREEK: John distinguishes two words for washing: "louō" (λούω, G3068) means to bathe the whole body, while "niptō" (νίπτω, G3538) means to wash a part. THEOLOGICAL: The imagery likely distinguishes the once-for-all cleansing of regeneration (the bath) from the ongoing cleansing of sanctification (the foot-washing). Believers who have been fully cleansed still need daily purification from the defilement of walking in the world.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: For He knew the one betraying Him; on account of this He said, "Not all of you are clean."
When therefore He had washed their feet and had taken His garments and reclined again, He said to them, "Do you know what I have done to you?
You call Me the Teacher and the Lord, and you speak rightly, for I am.
If therefore I, the Lord and the Teacher, washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another's feet.
For I gave you an example, that just as I did to you, you also should do.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Truly, truly, I say to you, a servant is not greater than his lord, nor is an apostle greater than the one who sent him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Apostolos" (ἀπόστολος, G652) here is used in its root sense of "one sent" rather than the later technical sense of the Twelve. This is the only occurrence of apostolos in John's Gospel. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus establishes a principle of representative authority that grounds the apostolic mission: the sent one bears the full authority and identity of the sender.

verses.flow: If you know these things, blessed are you if you do them.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I do not speak concerning all of you. I know whom I have chosen. But it is that the Scripture might be fulfilled: "The one eating my bread lifted up his heel against me."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Psalm 41:9 (LXX 40:10), a psalm of David concerning betrayal by a trusted companion. David's experience typologically prefigures Christ's betrayal by Judas. The phrase "lifted up his heel" (epēren tēn pternan) suggests a treacherous kick, as from a horse one has fed. GREEK: "Trōgōn" (τρώγων, G5176) is a vivid word meaning "to munch" or "gnaw" — denoting intimate table fellowship, intensifying the horror of the betrayal.

verses.flow: From now I tell you before it comes to pass, so that when it comes to pass you may believe that I AM.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Egō eimi" (ἐγώ εἰμι) — "I AM" — stands absolute without predicate, echoing the divine self-revelation of Exodus 3:14 (LXX: egō eimi ho ōn). Jesus claims that His foreknowledge of events, announced before they occur, should lead the disciples to recognize His identity as the God of Israel. This is one of John's most explicit divine identity claims for Jesus.

verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Truly, truly, I say to you, the one receiving whomever I send receives Me, and the one receiving Me receives the One who sent Me.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Having said these things, Jesus was troubled in the spirit and testified and said, "Truly, truly, I say to you that one of you will betray Me."

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: The disciples were looking at one another, being at a loss about whom He was speaking.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: There was reclining in the bosom of Jesus one of His disciples, whom Jesus loved.
Simon Peter therefore nods to this one to inquire who it might be about whom He speaks.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: That one, leaning back thus upon the breast of Jesus, says to Him, "Lord, who is it?"

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus answers, "It is that one for whom I shall dip the morsel and give it to him." Having dipped therefore the morsel, He gives it to Judas, son of Simon Iscariot.
And after the morsel, then Satan entered into that one. Jesus therefore says to him, "What you are doing, do more quickly."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The entrance of Satan into Judas after receiving the morsel marks the culmination of a progressive spiritual decline (cf. John 6:70; 12:6; 13:2). Jesus' command "Do more quickly" (poiēson tachion) demonstrates His sovereign authority even over the machinations of Satan — the betrayal proceeds not by Satan's initiative but under Christ's sovereign permission and timing. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Satan acts as adversary, yet even his rebellion serves the redemptive plan of God.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But no one of those reclining knew to what end He said this to him.
For some were supposing, since Judas had the money box, that Jesus was saying to him, "Buy what things we have need of for the feast," or that he should give something to the poor.
