words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Having therefore received the morsel, that one went out immediately. And it was night.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: "And it was night" (ēn de nyx) is one of the most theologically loaded narrative statements in John. On the surface it is a temporal note, but in John's light-darkness motif (cf. 1:5; 3:19; 8:12; 12:35-46), Judas' departure into the night symbolizes his passage into the realm of spiritual darkness and opposition to Christ, the Light of the world.

verses.flow: When therefore he had gone out, Jesus says, "Now the Son of Man has been glorified, and God has been glorified in Him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The aorist "edoxasthē" (ἐδοξάσθη) — "has been glorified" — treats the cross as an already-accomplished glorification. In John's theology, the cross is not merely suffering but the supreme revelation of divine glory. The mutual glorification of Father and Son reflects the intimate relationship within the Godhead (cf. John 17:1-5). TYPOLOGY: The Son of Man title draws on Daniel 7:13-14, where the one like a son of man receives glory and dominion from the Ancient of Days.

verses.flow: If God has been glorified in Him, God also will glorify Him in Himself, and will glorify Him immediately.
Little children, yet a little while I am with you. You will seek Me, and just as I said to the Jews, "Where I am going, you are not able to come," so now I say also to you.
A new commandment I give to you, that you love one another; just as I loved you, that you also love one another.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kainēn" (καινήν, G2537) means "new" in quality (not merely neos, "new in time"). The commandment to love is not novel in content (cf. Lev 19:18) but is new in its standard: "just as I loved you" (kathōs ēgapēsa hymas). Christ's self-sacrificial love becomes the measure and model. THEOLOGICAL: This "new commandment" becomes the defining mark of the new covenant community, replacing ethnic and ritual markers with cruciform love as the badge of discipleship.

verses.flow: By this all shall know that you are My disciples, if you have love among one another."
Simon Peter says to Him, "Lord, where are You going?" Jesus answered, "Where I am going you are not able to follow Me now, but you shall follow afterward."
Peter says to Him, "Lord, why am I not able to follow You now? I will lay down my life for You."
Jesus answers, "Will you lay down your life for Me? Truly, truly, I say to you, the rooster shall by no means crow until you deny Me three times."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Roman division of the night included a watch called "alektorophōnia" (cock-crowing), roughly 12:00-3:00 AM. Peter's threefold denial (cf. John 18:17, 25-27) would occur before dawn. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' precise foreknowledge of Peter's failure, stated with the solemn double-amen formula, demonstrates both His omniscience and His grace — He predicts the failure yet does not reject Peter, anticipating his restoration (cf. John 21:15-17).

verses.flow: Let not your heart be troubled; you believe in God, believe also in me.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb ταρασσέσθω (tarassesthō, Strong's G5015) is a present passive imperative—"stop being agitated/stirred up." The same verb describes the troubling of water at Bethesda (John 5:4) and Jesus' own spirit (John 11:33; 13:21). The dual imperatives πιστεύετε can be read as indicative + imperative: "You believe in God; believe also in me"—placing Jesus on the same plane of trust as God himself.

verses.flow: In the house of my Father are many dwelling places; and if not, I would have told you, for I go to prepare a place for you.
verses.note: GREEK: μοναί (monai, Strong's G3438) appears only here and in v.23 in the NT—a true Johannine near-hapax. It derives from μένω (menō, "to abide/remain"), the signature Johannine verb. These are not temporary inns but permanent dwelling places. TYPOLOGY: The "house of my Father" echoes the Temple language of John 2:16, yet here transcends the earthly Temple—the Father's house is his eternal domain where the Son prepares a place, fulfilling the tabernacling presence of God with his people (Exod 25:8; Rev 21:3).

verses.flow: And if I go and prepare a place for you, I am coming again and will receive you to myself, that where I am, you also may be.
And where I am going, you know the way.
Thomas says to him, Lord, we do not know where you are going; how are we able to know the way?
Jesus says to him, I am the way, and the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The emphatic ἐγώ εἰμι (egō eimi) formula echoes the divine self-revelation of Exodus 3:14 (LXX). Jesus claims exclusive mediation to the Father through three articular nouns: ἡ ὁδός (the way), ἡ ἀλήθεια (the truth), ἡ ζωή (the life). The definite articles are crucial—he is not a way but the way. TYPOLOGY: Christ fulfills the "way" of access to God's presence that the tabernacle/Temple system prefigured (Heb 10:19-20). The exclusivity clause (εἰ μὴ δι' ἐμοῦ) admits no alternative mediator.

verses.flow: If you had known me, you would have known my Father also; from now on you know him, and you have seen him.
Philip says to him, Lord, show us the Father, and it is sufficient for us.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus says to him, So long a time I am with you, and you have not known me, Philip? The one having seen me has seen the Father; how do you say, Show us the Father?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' response to Philip constitutes one of the highest Christological statements in the Fourth Gospel. "The one having seen me has seen the Father" (ὁ ἑωρακὼς ἐμὲ ἑώρακεν τὸν πατέρα) asserts not mere representation but ontological unity—to perceive Jesus is to perceive the Father himself. This corresponds to the Prologue's claim that the Logos was πρὸς τὸν θεόν and was θεός (John 1:1), and that the Son is the ἐξηγέομαι ("exegesis") of the unseen Father (John 1:18).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Do you not believe that I am in the Father, and the Father is in me? The words that I speak to you, I speak not from myself; but the Father abiding in me, he does his works.
Believe me that I am in the Father, and the Father in me; but if not, believe on account of the works themselves.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Truly, truly, I say to you, the one believing in me, the works that I do, that one also shall do, and greater than these shall he do, because I am going to the Father.
And whatever you ask in my name, this I will do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son.
If you ask me anything in my name, I will do it.
If you love me, you will keep my commandments.
And I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Advocate, that he may be with you unto the age—
verses.note: GREEK: παράκλητος (paraklētos, Strong's G3875) means "one called alongside"—an advocate, intercessor, or counselor. The word ἄλλον (allon, "another of the same kind") rather than ἕτερον (heteron, "another of a different kind") implies the Spirit is an Advocate of the same nature as Jesus himself. THEOLOGICAL: This is the first of five Paraclete sayings in the Farewell Discourse (14:16-17, 14:26, 15:26, 16:7-11, 16:13-15). The phrase εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα ("unto the age") indicates the Spirit's permanent indwelling, contrasting the temporary presence of Christ in the flesh.

verses.flow: the Spirit of truth, whom the world is not able to receive, because it does not behold him nor know him; you know him, because he abides with you and shall be in you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας ("the Spirit of truth") identifies the Paraclete as a personal divine agent—not an impersonal force. The neuter pronoun ὅ follows grammatical gender of πνεῦμα, but the masculine ἐκεῖνος in v.26 reveals personal identity. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Spirit's role as revealer of truth parallels the heavenly council function where God's sôd (secret counsel) is disclosed to his servants (Amos 3:7; Jer 23:18). The world's inability to receive him marks an epistemological divide between those in covenant and those outside.

verses.flow: I will not leave you as orphans; I am coming to you.
Yet a little while, and the world beholds me no longer; but you behold me, because I live, and you also shall live.
In that day you shall know that I am in my Father, and you in me, and I in you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The mutual indwelling formula—"I in my Father, you in me, I in you"—presents a three-level participation: the Son in the Father, believers in the Son, and the Son in believers. This perichoretic language anticipates the high priestly prayer of John 17:21-23 and establishes that union with Christ is union with the Godhead. GREEK: γνώσεσθε (gnōsesthe, future middle of γινώσκω) indicates experiential, relational knowledge—not mere intellectual awareness.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one having my commandments and keeping them, that one it is who loves me; and the one loving me shall be loved by my Father, and I will love him and will manifest myself to him.
Judas—not the Iscariot—says to him, Lord, what has come about that you are about to manifest yourself to us and not to the world?
Jesus answered and said to him, If anyone loves me, he will keep my word, and my Father will love him, and we will come to him and will make our dwelling place with him.
verses.note: GREEK: μονήν (monēn) echoes v.2 (μοναί), creating an inclusio: the Father's house has many dwelling places for believers, and reciprocally the Father and Son make their dwelling place with the one who loves and obeys. THEOLOGICAL: The first-person plural ἐλευσόμεθα ("we will come") and ποιησόμεθα ("we will make") is remarkable—the Father and Son together personally indwell the believer. This fulfills the covenant promise: "I will dwell in their midst" (Exod 29:45; Ezek 37:27).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: The one not loving me does not keep my words; and the word that you hear is not mine, but the Father's who sent me.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: These things I have spoken to you while abiding with you.
But the Advocate, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, that one will teach you all things and will bring to your remembrance all things that I said to you.
verses.note: GREEK: ὑπομνήσει (hypomnēsei, from ὑπομιμνῄσκω, Strong's G5279) means "to bring to remembrance, to recall to mind." This grounds apostolic testimony in divine superintendence—the Spirit ensures accurate remembrance and transmission of Jesus' teaching. THEOLOGICAL: The Holy Spirit is sent by the Father ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου ("in my name"), meaning he comes with the full authority and mission of the Son. This Trinitarian sending pattern—Father sends, in the Son's name, the Spirit as agent—structures the economy of salvation.

verses.flow: Peace I leave to you; my peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give to you. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid.
You heard that I said to you, I am going away and I am coming to you. If you loved me, you would have rejoiced that I am going to the Father, because the Father is greater than I.
And now I have told you before it comes to pass, that when it comes to pass, you may believe.
No longer will I speak much with you, for the ruler of the world is coming, and in me he has nothing.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: ὁ τοῦ κόσμου ἄρχων ("the ruler of the world") identifies Satan as the hostile cosmic authority over the present world-order (cf. John 12:31; 16:11; 2 Cor 4:4; Eph 2:2). The phrase ἐν ἐμοὶ οὐκ ἔχει οὐδέν ("in me he has nothing") declares Jesus' complete sinlessness and immunity from Satan's dominion—there is no foothold, no claim, no jurisdiction. THEOLOGICAL: This frames the crucifixion not as Satan's victory but as the arena of his defeat, since the one going to the cross owes nothing to the accuser.

verses.flow: But that the world may know that I love the Father, and just as the Father commanded me, so I do. Arise, let us go from here.
I am the vine, the true one, and my Father is the husbandman.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ampelos" (ἄμπελος, Strong's G288) means "vine," and "alēthinē" (ἀληθινή, G228) means "true" or "genuine," distinguishing the real from the apparent. "Geōrgos" (γεωργός, G1092) means "farmer" or "vinedresser," one who tends cultivated ground. TYPOLOGY: Israel is repeatedly called God's vine in the OT (Isa 5:1-7; Ps 80:8-16; Jer 2:21), yet proved unfaithful. Jesus declares himself the "true vine," the faithful Israel, fulfilling what the nation failed to be. This is the last of the Johannine "I am" (egō eimi) declarations, echoing the divine name (Exod 3:14).

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular neuter
verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: Every branch in me not bearing fruit, he takes away; and every one bearing fruit, he prunes it, that it may bear more fruit.
verses.note: GREEK: "Airei" (αἴρει, G142) can mean "takes away" or "lifts up" — some argue the vinedresser lifts fallen branches before cutting. "Kathairei" (καθαίρει, G2508) means "prunes" or "cleanses," sharing the same root as "katharos" (clean) in v3, creating a deliberate wordplay. The purpose clause with "hina" (ἵνα) indicates divine intentionality in the pruning.

verses.flow: Already you are clean through the word which I have spoken to you.
verses.note: GREEK: "Katharoi" (καθαροί, G2513) means "clean" or "pure," connecting to the pruning (kathairō) of v2 through shared etymology. The cleansing agent is "the word" (ton logon), recalling John 1:1 where Christ himself is the Logos. THEOLOGICAL: The disciples' cleansing is already accomplished (perfect tense "lelālēka," I have spoken), indicating the efficacy of Christ's word as a completed reality.

verses.flow: Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch is not able to bear fruit of itself unless it abides in the vine, so neither can you unless you abide in me.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I am the vine, you are the branches. The one abiding in me, and I in him, this one bears much fruit; for apart from me you are able to do nothing.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Apart from me you are able to do nothing" (chōris emou ou dynasthe poiein ouden) is among the strongest statements of absolute dependence on Christ in the New Testament. This verse demolishes any notion of autonomous spiritual productivity. The emphatic "I am the vine, you are the branches" establishes an organic, vital union — not merely moral example but ontological connection.

verses.flow: Unless someone abides in me, he is cast out as the branch and is withered; and they gather them and cast them into the fire, and they are burned.
If you abide in me and my words abide in you, ask whatever you desire, and it shall come to pass for you.
In this is my Father glorified, that you bear much fruit and become my disciples.
verses.note: GREEK: "Edoxasthē" (ἐδοξάσθη) is an aorist passive — "was glorified" — used with a timeless or proleptic force. The Father's glory is realized in the fruit-bearing of disciples. THEOLOGICAL: Discipleship is not merely following but fruit-bearing; "genēsthe emoi mathētai" (become my disciples) suggests an ongoing process of proving to be genuine disciples through fruitfulness (Matt 7:16-20).

verses.flow: As the Father has loved me, I also have loved you. Abide in my love.
If you keep my commandments, you shall abide in my love, even as I have kept the commandments of my Father and abide in his love.
These things I have spoken to you, that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be made full.
This is my commandment, that you love one another, even as I have loved you.
Greater love than this no one has, that someone should lay down his life for his friends.
verses.note: GREEK: "Psychē" (ψυχή, G5590) here means "life" or "soul" — the totality of one's earthly existence. "Thē" (θῇ) from "tithēmi" (G5087) means "to lay down" or "to place," used also in John 10:11, 15, 17-18 of the Good Shepherd laying down his life. TYPOLOGY: This verse anticipates Christ's own sacrificial death on the cross, defining the supreme expression of love. What he commands in v12, he himself will fulfill at Golgotha.

verses.flow: You are my friends if you do what I command you.
No longer do I call you servants, for the servant does not know what his lord does; but I have called you friends, for all things which I heard from my Father I have made known to you.
verses.note: GREEK: "Doulous" (δούλους, G1401) means "slaves" or "bondservants," those with no inherent right to know the master's will. "Philous" (φίλους, G5384) means "friends," denoting intimacy and equality of standing. THEOLOGICAL: This represents a radical elevation of status. In the ancient Near East and Second Temple context, being called a king's "friend" was a title of highest honor (cf. Abraham as "friend of God," Isa 41:8; Jas 2:23). Jesus reveals the Father's full counsel to those he calls friends.

verses.flow: You did not choose me, but I chose you and appointed you, that you should go and bear fruit, and that your fruit should abide, so that whatever you ask the Father in my name, he may give to you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Divine election is underscored — "I chose you" (egō exelexamēn hymas), not human initiative, is the ground of discipleship. "Ethēka" (ἔθηκα, G5087) means "I appointed" or "I set/placed," indicating commissioning with authority. The fruit that "abides" (menē) connects back to the vine metaphor, indicating lasting, enduring spiritual produce. The prayer promise is grounded in election, mission, and the authority of Jesus' name.

verses.flow: These things I command you, that you love one another.
If the world hates you, know that it has hated me before you.
If you were of the world, the world would love its own; but because you are not of the world, but I chose you out of the world, on account of this the world hates you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The threefold repetition of "kosmos" (κόσμος, G2889) — the world — emphasizes the antithesis between the kingdom of God and the present world-system opposed to God. Election ("I chose you out of the world") simultaneously explains both the world's hatred and the disciples' alien status. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The concept of being chosen "out of the world" echoes the cosmic separation between those who belong to God's assembly and the rebellious world order under the dominion of hostile spiritual powers (cf. John 12:31; 1 John 5:19; Eph 2:2).

verses.flow: Remember the word which I said to you: A servant is not greater than his lord. If they persecuted me, they will also persecute you; if they kept my word, they will keep yours also.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But all these things they will do to you on account of my name, because they do not know the one who sent me.
If I had not come and spoken to them, they would not have sin; but now they have no excuse concerning their sin.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one hating me hates my Father also.
If I had not done among them the works which no one else did, they would not have sin; but now they have both seen and hated both me and my Father.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But this is so that the word written in their law might be fulfilled: "They hated me without a cause."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Psalm 35:19 or Psalm 69:4 (LXX 68:5) — "They hated me without a cause" (emisēsan me dōrean). Jesus identifies "their law" (the Torah and Writings) as testifying against those who reject him. The causeless hatred of the righteous sufferer in the Psalms finds its ultimate fulfillment in the hatred directed at Christ. GREEK: "Dōrean" (δωρεάν, G1432) means "freely, without cause, gratuitously" — the hatred has no legitimate ground.

verses.flow: When the Advocate comes, whom I will send to you from the Father — the Spirit of Truth, who proceeds from the Father — that one will bear witness concerning me.
verses.note: GREEK: "Paraklētos" (παράκλητος, G3875) means "Advocate," "Helper," or "one called alongside." It is a legal term for a counsel for the defense. "Ekporeuetai" (ἐκπορεύεται, G1607) — "proceeds" — is present tense, indicating an eternal, ongoing procession from the Father. THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit of Truth is sent by the Son from the Father and proceeds from the Father — a crucial text for Trinitarian theology. The Spirit's primary mission here is christological: "he will bear witness concerning me." DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Spirit as a divine person proceeding from the Father and sent by the Son reflects the inner life of the Godhead and the divine council's unified testimony to Christ's identity and work.

verses.flow: And you also bear witness, because you have been with me from the beginning.
These things I have spoken to you, that you may not be caused to stumble.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: They will put you out of the synagogues; but an hour is coming that everyone who kills you will think he is offering service to God.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀποσυναγώγους (aposynagōgous, Strong's G656) is a compound adjective meaning "expelled from the synagogue." This term appears only in John's Gospel (9:22; 12:42; 16:2), making it virtually a Johannine technical term. HISTORICAL: Formal expulsion from the synagogue (birkat ha-minim) became a defining crisis for Jewish believers in Jesus, likely intensifying after 70 AD but with precedents in earlier community discipline. The claim that killing believers constitutes λατρείαν (latreian, "sacred service/worship") to God reflects zealous persecution modeled on Phinehas (Num 25:11-13).

verses.flow: And these things they will do because they have not known the Father, nor me.
But these things I have spoken to you, that when their hour comes, you may remember them, that I told you. And these things I did not say to you from the beginning, because I was with you.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But now I go away to the one who sent me, and none of you asks me, "Where are you going?"
But because I have spoken these things to you, sorrow has filled your heart.
But I tell you the truth: it is to your advantage that I go away; for if I do not go away, the Advocate will by no means come to you; but if I go, I will send him to you.
verses.note: GREEK: παράκλητος (paraklētos, Strong's G3875) literally means "one called alongside," used in legal contexts for an advocate or intercessor. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus presents his departure as soteriologically necessary — the sending of the Spirit is contingent upon the completed work of Christ's death, resurrection, and ascension. The strong double negative οὐ μὴ ἔλθῃ emphasizes the impossibility of the Spirit's coming apart from Christ's glorification (cf. John 7:39).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having come, that one will convict the world concerning sin, and concerning righteousness, and concerning judgment:
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit's threefold work of ἐλέγξει (elenxei, "convicting/exposing") addresses the world's fundamental errors regarding sin, righteousness, and judgment. This is forensic and cosmic language — the Advocate functions as divine prosecutor against the world system that rejected the Messiah. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Spirit acts as the heavenly court's agent of conviction, executing the verdict rendered by Christ's cross and resurrection.

verses.flow: concerning sin, because they do not believe in me;
concerning righteousness, because I go away to the Father, and you behold me no longer;
and concerning judgment, because the ruler of this world has been judged.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: ὁ ἄρχων τοῦ κόσμου τούτου (ho archōn tou kosmou toutou, "the ruler of this world") identifies Satan as the chief hostile spiritual power over the present age. The perfect tense κέκριται (kekritai, "has been judged") indicates that the verdict against this ruler is already rendered and stands — the cross is the decisive judgment of the hostile powers (cf. Col 2:15; John 12:31). TYPOLOGY: This echoes the divine council judgment scene in which rebellious elohim are stripped of authority (Ps 82:1-7).

verses.flow: I still have many things to say to you, but you are not able to bear them now.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: But when that one comes, the Spirit of truth, he will guide you in all the truth; for he will not speak from himself, but whatever he hears he will speak, and the things that are coming he will declare to you.
verses.note: GREEK: ὁδηγήσει (hodēgēsei, "he will guide") derives from ὁδός (hodos, "way") + ἡγέομαι (hēgeomai, "to lead"), evoking God leading Israel through the wilderness. THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit of truth does not speak ἀφ' ἑαυτοῦ ("from himself") but speaks only what he hears — this establishes the Spirit's ministry as derivative from and perfectly aligned with the Father and the Son, reflecting intra-Trinitarian harmony. The Spirit also ἀναγγελεῖ ("will declare") τὰ ἐρχόμενα ("the things coming"), indicating a prophetic and eschatological dimension to his ministry.

verses.flow: That one will glorify me, because he will take from what is mine and will declare it to you.
All things that the Father has are mine; for this reason I said that he takes from what is mine and will declare it to you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: πάντα ὅσα ἔχει ὁ πατὴρ ἐμά ἐστιν ("All things that the Father has are mine") is a breathtaking claim of co-equal divine possession — the Son shares fully in all the Father's prerogatives and attributes. This grounds the Spirit's revelatory ministry: since everything belonging to the Father belongs equally to the Son, the Spirit draws from one undivided divine treasury. This verse is among the strongest implicit declarations of the Son's full deity in the Fourth Gospel (cf. John 17:10).

verses.flow: A little while, and you no longer behold me; and again a little while, and you will see me.

Then some of his disciples said to one another, "What is this that he says to us, 'A little while, and you do not behold me, and again a little while, and you will see me,' and, 'Because I go away to the Father'?"
Therefore they were saying, "What is this 'a little while'? We do not know what he is speaking."

Jesus knew that they were wanting to ask him, and he said to them, "Concerning this you inquire with one another, that I said, 'A little while, and you do not behold me, and again a little while, and you will see me'?
Truly, truly, I say to you, that you will weep and will lament, but the world will rejoice; you will be sorrowful, but your sorrow will become joy.
verses.note: GREEK: The ἀμὴν ἀμὴν (amēn amēn) double-amen formula is unique to John's Gospel and marks solemn, authoritative pronouncements of Jesus. TYPOLOGY: The pattern of sorrow transformed into joy echoes the prophetic pattern of Israel's exile and restoration (Isa 66:14; Jer 31:13). The world's rejoicing at the disciples' sorrow inverts at the resurrection.

verses.flow: The woman, when she gives birth, has sorrow, because her hour has come; but when she has brought forth the child, she no longer remembers the affliction, on account of the joy that a man has been born into the world.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The imagery of a woman in travail (ὠδίνουσα) is deeply rooted in OT prophetic literature as a metaphor for eschatological suffering preceding divine deliverance (Isa 26:17-19; 66:7-9; Mic 4:9-10). THEOLOGICAL: The birth of ἄνθρωπος ("a man/human") into the world may carry deeper Christological resonance — the new creation reality born through Christ's death and resurrection. The hour (ὥρα) of the woman parallels Jesus' own "hour" throughout John's Gospel.

verses.flow: And you therefore now indeed have sorrow; but I will see you again, and your heart will rejoice, and your joy no one takes from you.
And in that day you will ask me nothing. Truly, truly, I say to you, whatever you ask the Father in my name, he will give you.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Until now you have asked nothing in my name. Ask, and you will receive, that your joy may be made full.
These things I have spoken to you in figures of speech; an hour is coming when I will no longer speak to you in figures of speech, but with boldness concerning the Father I will declare to you.
In that day you will ask in my name, and I do not say to you that I will make request of the Father concerning you;
for the Father himself loves you, because you have loved me and have believed that I came forth from God.
I came forth from the Father and have come into the world; again, I leave the world and go to the Father.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse presents the most concise summary of the entire Johannine Christological narrative: pre-existent origin from the Father, incarnational entry into the world, departure from the world, and return to the Father. STRUCTURE: The chiastic pattern (came forth from Father → came into world → leave world → go to Father) mirrors the great descent-ascent Christology of Philippians 2:6-11 and John's Prologue (1:1-18).

verses.flow: His disciples say, "Behold, now you speak with boldness, and you speak no figure of speech.
Now we know that you know all things, and have no need that anyone should question you; by this we believe that you came forth from God."
Jesus answered them, "Do you now believe?
Behold, an hour is coming, and has come, that you will be scattered, each to his own, and you will leave me alone; and yet I am not alone, because the Father is with me.
These things I have spoken to you, that in me you may have peace. In the world you have affliction; but take courage — I have overcome the world.
verses.note: GREEK: θαρσεῖτε (tharseite, "take courage/be of good cheer," Strong's G2293) is an imperative — a command, not mere encouragement. νενίκηκα (nenikēka) is a perfect tense of νικάω — "I have overcome" — indicating a completed victory with abiding results. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus speaks of his victory over the world (τὸν κόσμον) as already accomplished (perfect tense), even before the cross, because the outcome is certain in the divine purpose. This forms a theological inclusio with 16:1 — the discourse began to prevent stumbling and ends with the assurance of Christ's cosmic triumph. TYPOLOGY: The promise of εἰρήνη ("peace") in the midst of θλῖψις ("affliction/tribulation") echoes the eschatological shalom promised through the Messiah (Isa 9:6-7; 53:5).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: These things Jesus spoke, and lifting up his eyes to heaven, he said, "Father, the hour has come; glorify your Son, that the Son may glorify you,
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἐπάρας τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς" (eparas tous ophthalmous) — "lifting up the eyes" is a Semitic posture of prayer directed toward heaven (cf. Ps 123:1). "Ἡ ὥρα" (hē hōra, Strong's G5610) — "the hour" is the appointed, culminating hour of Jesus' glorification through crucifixion, resurrection, and ascension, a theme threaded throughout John's Gospel (John 2:4; 7:30; 12:23; 13:1). THEOLOGICAL: This entire chapter is often called the "High Priestly Prayer," in which Jesus intercedes as both Son and mediator, fulfilling the priestly typology of Melchizedek and Aaron simultaneously.

verses.flow: even as you gave him authority over all flesh, that all which you have given him, he might give to them eternal life.
And this is eternal life, that they may know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus defines eternal life not as mere duration but as relational knowledge (γινώσκω, ginōskō, Strong's G1097) — experiential, covenantal knowing of the only true God and of Jesus Christ whom he sent. The juxtaposition of "the only true God" and "Jesus Christ" within a single purpose clause places the Son alongside the Father as the necessary object of saving knowledge, without collapsing their distinction. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "τὸν μόνον ἀληθινὸν θεόν" (the only true God) distinguishes the Most High from all other elohim-class beings in the divine council (cf. Deut 32:8-9 LXX; Ps 82:1), affirming absolute monotheism within a context that acknowledges the reality of spiritual beings.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I glorified you upon the earth, having completed the work which you have given me that I should do.
And now, glorify me, you, Father, with yourself, with the glory which I had with you before the world was.
verses.note: GREEK: "Τῇ δόξῃ ᾗ εἶχον πρὸ τοῦ τὸν κόσμον εἶναι παρὰ σοί" — "with the glory which I had before the world was, with you." The imperfect εἶχον (eichon) indicates continuous possession of glory in the pre-existent state. THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the clearest Johannine statements of the pre-existence of Christ. Jesus asks to be restored to the shared glory he possessed with the Father before creation (cf. John 1:1-3; Phil 2:6-8; Col 1:15-17). The preposition παρά + dative ("with/alongside you") denotes intimate, face-to-face presence — personal distinction within shared divine glory.

verses.flow: I manifested your name to the men whom you gave me out of the world. Yours they were, and to me you gave them, and your word they have kept.
Now they have come to know that all things, as many as you have given me, are from you;
for the words which you gave me I have given to them, and they received them and knew truly that I came forth from you, and they believed that you sent me.
I ask concerning them; not concerning the world do I ask, but concerning those whom you have given me, for they are yours.
And all things that are mine are yours, and yours are mine, and I have been glorified in them.
And no longer am I in the world, yet they are in the world, and I am coming to you. Holy Father, keep them in your name which you have given me, that they may be one, even as we are.
verses.note: GREEK: "Πάτερ ἅγιε" (Pater hagie) — "Holy Father" occurs only here in the NT. The adjective ἅγιος (hagios, Strong's G40) applied to the Father emphasizes his transcendent separation and moral purity, forming a basis for the petition to "keep" (τήρησον, tērēson) the disciples. THEOLOGICAL: The prayer for unity — "that they may be one even as we are" — grounds ecclesial unity in the intra-Trinitarian relationship between Father and Son, not in mere organizational agreement. This unity is ontological in archetype and covenantal in expression.

verses.flow: When I was with them, I was keeping them in your name which you have given me, and I guarded them, and none of them perished except the son of destruction, that the Scripture might be fulfilled.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ὁ υἱὸς τῆς ἀπωλείας" (ho huios tēs apōleias) — "the son of destruction" is a Semitic idiom meaning one characterized by or destined for perdition. The same phrase appears in 2 Thess 2:3 for the eschatological "man of lawlessness." TYPOLOGY: The reference to Scripture being fulfilled likely points to Ps 41:9 (LXX 40:10) and possibly Ps 109:8, linking Judas' betrayal to the pattern of the treacherous companion of the righteous sufferer (cf. John 13:18; Acts 1:16-20).

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: But now I am coming to you, and these things I speak in the world, that they may have my joy fulfilled in themselves.
I have given them your word, and the world hated them, because they are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.
I do not ask that you take them out of the world, but that you keep them from the evil one.
They are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.
Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἁγίασον" (hagiason, aorist imperative of ἁγιάζω, hagiazō, Strong's G37) — "sanctify" or "set apart," carrying both cultic consecration and moral purification. THEOLOGICAL: The instrument of sanctification is truth (ἀλήθεια, alētheia), and Jesus immediately identifies the Father's word (λόγος) as that truth. Since Jesus himself is the Logos (John 1:1) and "the truth" (John 14:6), the Father's word and the incarnate Son converge as the sanctifying reality for believers.

verses.flow: Even as you sent me into the world, I also sent them into the world.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And on behalf of them I sanctify myself, that they also may be sanctified in truth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "I sanctify myself" (ἁγιάζω ἐμαυτόν) carries sacrificial and priestly overtones — Jesus consecrates himself as the offering, so that his disciples might be truly sanctified. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the OT consecration of priests and sacrificial animals (Ex 28:41; Lev 22:2-3). Christ fulfills both the role of the consecrating priest and the consecrated offering, the antitype of every Levitical sacrifice (cf. Heb 9:14; 10:10).

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And not concerning these only do I ask, but also concerning those who believe in me through their word,
that they all may be one, even as you, Father, are in me and I in you, that they also may be in us, that the world may believe that you sent me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The unity of believers is modeled on the mutual indwelling of Father and Son ("as you, Father, are in me and I in you") and has a missional purpose: "that the world may believe that you sent me." The oneness is thus both mystical (participation in the divine life) and evidential (a witness to the world). STRUCTURE: Three ἵνα (hina, "that/so that") clauses cascade here: unity among believers → their inclusion in the Father-Son relationship → the world's belief. This reveals the evangelistic dimension of Trinitarian community.

verses.flow: And the glory which you have given me, I have given to them, that they may be one, even as we are one—

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: I in them and you in me—that they may be perfected into one, that the world may know that you sent me, and loved them even as you loved me.
Father, that which you have given me, I desire that where I am, they also may be with me, that they may behold my glory which you have given me, for you loved me before the foundation of the world.
verses.note: GREEK: "Πρὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου" (pro katabolēs kosmou) — "before the foundation of the world." Καταβολή (katabolē, Strong's G2602) literally means "casting down" or "laying the foundation." THEOLOGICAL: The Father's love for the Son is pre-creational and eternal, not contingent on the created order. This statement, combined with v.5, affirms that the Son's glory and the Father's love precede all creation, anchoring the economy of salvation in the eternal counsel of God (cf. Eph 1:4; 1 Pet 1:20; Rev 13:8).

verses.flow: Righteous Father, indeed the world did not know you, but I knew you, and these knew that you sent me.
And I made known to them your name, and will make it known, that the love with which you loved me may be in them, and I in them."
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἐγνώρισα... καὶ γνωρίσω" (egnōrisa... kai gnōrisō) — "I made known... and will make known." The aorist followed by the future tense indicates that the revelation of the Father's name is both accomplished in Christ's earthly ministry and ongoing through the Spirit's work after his departure. THEOLOGICAL: The prayer concludes with the ultimate purpose of all revelation: that the very love the Father has for the Son would indwell believers, and that Christ himself would be "in them" — the fullest expression of union with God. This is the culmination of Johannine theology: divine love, divine knowledge, and divine indwelling converging in the redeemed community.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Having said these things, Jesus went out with His disciples beyond the winter-torrent of the Kedron, where there was a garden, into which He entered, He Himself and His disciples.
verses.note: GREEK: The term χειμάρρου (cheimarrou, "winter-torrent") refers to a wadi that flows only in rainy season. Κεδρών (Kedron) is the brook Kidron, east of Jerusalem between the city and the Mount of Olives. TYPOLOGY: As David crossed the Kidron fleeing Absalom's betrayal (2 Sam 15:23), so Jesus crosses it facing Judas's betrayal — the true Son of David fulfilling the pattern of the rejected king.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now Judas also, the one betraying Him, knew the place, because many times Jesus had gathered there with His disciples.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Judas then, having received the cohort and officers from the chief priests and from the Pharisees, comes there with lanterns and torches and weapons.
verses.note: GREEK: σπεῖραν (speiran, Strong's G4686) technically denotes a Roman cohort (typically 600 soldiers), indicating Pilate had authorized Roman military involvement in the arrest alongside the temple police (ὑπηρέτας, hypēretas). HISTORICAL: The combined presence of Roman soldiers and Jewish temple officers reveals the coordination between Roman and Jewish authorities against Jesus.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: Jesus therefore, knowing all the things coming upon Him, went forth and says to them, "Whom do you seek?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: They answered Him, "Jesus the Nazarene." Jesus says to them, "I am." And Judas also, the one betraying Him, was standing with them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase ἐγώ εἰμι (egō eimi, "I am") carries immense weight in John's Gospel, echoing the divine self-revelation of God to Moses at the burning bush (Exod 3:14, LXX). Jesus uses it here not merely as identification but as a divine self-declaration, which explains the supernatural response in v. 6.

verses.flow: When therefore He said to them, "I am," they drew back and fell to the ground.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The falling backward (ἀπῆλθον εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω καὶ ἔπεσαν χαμαί) at the spoken "I am" demonstrates that Jesus's arrest was voluntary, not forced. This echoes Old Testament theophanies where the divine presence overwhelms those who encounter it (Ezek 1:28; Dan 10:9). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The sovereign power displayed here reveals the divine authority of the Son — those who come to seize the Lord of glory are prostrated by His mere word.

verses.flow: Again therefore He asked them, "Whom do you seek?" And they said, "Jesus the Nazarene."
Jesus answered, "I told you that I am. If therefore you seek Me, let these go away,"
in order that the word might be fulfilled which He spoke: "Of those whom You have given Me, I lost not one of them."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus quotes His own words from John 17:12 as though they were Scripture requiring fulfillment (ἵνα πληρωθῇ), placing His own words on the level of prophetic utterance. THEOLOGICAL: The Good Shepherd lays down His life for the sheep but ensures not one is lost — His substitutionary surrender protects the flock (John 10:11, 28).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Simon Peter therefore, having a sword, drew it and struck the servant of the high priest and cut off his right ear. Now the name of the servant was Malchus.
Jesus therefore said to Peter, "Put the sword into the sheath. The cup which the Father has given Me, shall I not surely drink it?"
verses.note: GREEK: θήκην (thēkēn, Strong's G2336) means "sheath" or "case" — a hapax legomenon in the NT. TYPOLOGY: The "cup" (ποτήριον, potērion) which the Father has given recalls Jesus's Gethsemane prayer (Matt 26:39) and the prophetic cup of divine judgment (Isa 51:17, 22; Jer 25:15). Jesus willingly receives the cup of wrath that was destined for sinful humanity.

verses.flow: The cohort therefore, and the commander, and the officers of the Jews seized Jesus and bound Him,
and led Him to Annas first, for he was father-in-law of Caiaphas, who was high priest that year.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now it was Caiaphas who had counseled the Jews that it is expedient for one man to die on behalf of the people.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Caiaphas's counsel (John 11:49-52) that one man should die ὑπὲρ τοῦ λαοῦ (hyper tou laou, "on behalf of the people") was an unwitting prophecy. John's ironic theology shows the high priest unknowingly articulating the substitutionary atonement of Christ — the true High Priest offering Himself as the sacrifice.

verses.flow: And Simon Peter was following Jesus, and so was another disciple. Now that disciple was known to the high priest, and he entered with Jesus into the courtyard of the high priest,
but Peter was standing at the door outside. Therefore the other disciple, the one known to the high priest, went out and spoke to the doorkeeper and brought Peter in.
The servant girl, the doorkeeper, therefore says to Peter, "Are not you also one of this man's disciples?" That one says, "I am not."

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now the servants and the officers were standing, having made a charcoal fire, because it was cold, and they were warming themselves. And Peter also was with them, standing and warming himself.
The high priest therefore questioned Jesus concerning His disciples and concerning His teaching.
Jesus answered him, "I have spoken openly to the world. I always taught in the synagogue and in the temple, where all the Jews come together, and in secret I spoke nothing.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: "Why do you question Me? Question those who have heard what I spoke to them. Behold, these know what things I said."

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And when He had said these things, one of the officers standing by gave Jesus a strike with the palm, saying, "Is this how You answer the high priest?"
Jesus answered him, "If I spoke wrongly, bear witness concerning the wrong; but if rightly, why do you strike Me?"

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Annas therefore sent Him, having been bound, to Caiaphas the high priest.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The sending of Jesus bound (δεδεμένον) from Annas to Caiaphas reflects the irregular nature of this trial. Annas, though deposed by Rome in AD 15, retained enormous influence as patriarch of the high-priestly family. The formal high priest was Caiaphas, his son-in-law, yet Annas conducted the preliminary interrogation — revealing the corruption of the priestly establishment.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now Simon Peter was standing and warming himself. They said therefore to him, "Are not you also one of His disciples?" That one denied it and said, "I am not."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: One of the servants of the high priest, being a kinsman of the one whose ear Peter cut off, says, "Did I not see you in the garden with Him?"
Peter therefore denied again, and immediately a rooster crowed.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Peter's triple denial fulfills Jesus's prophecy (John 13:38) with precise accuracy. The rooster's crow (ἀλέκτωρ ἐφώνησεν) marks the completion of the pattern and stands in stark contrast to Jesus's bold "I am" declarations in the same chapter. Where the Lord stands firm, the disciple falls.

verses.flow: They lead Jesus therefore from Caiaphas to the Praetorium. Now it was early morning. And they themselves did not enter into the Praetorium, so that they would not be defiled, but might eat the Passover.
verses.note: GREEK: πραιτώριον (praitōrion, Strong's G4232) is a Latin loanword designating the Roman governor's official residence, likely the former palace of Herod the Great or the Antonia Fortress. HISTORICAL: The Jews' refusal to enter the Praetorium to avoid ritual defilement (μιανθῶσιν, mianthōsin) before eating the Passover presents profound irony — they observe ceremonial purity while delivering the innocent Lamb of God to death. TYPOLOGY: The timing with the Passover (πάσχα) confirms Jesus as the true Paschal Lamb whose blood delivers from judgment (Exod 12:1-13; 1 Cor 5:7).

verses.flow: Pilate therefore went out to them and says, "What accusation do you bring against this man?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: They answered and said to him, "If this one were not doing evil, we would not have delivered Him to you."
Pilate therefore said to them, "Take Him yourselves and judge Him according to your law." The Jews said to him, "It is not lawful for us to put anyone to death,"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: in order that the word of Jesus might be fulfilled, which He spoke signifying by what kind of death He was about to die.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Jews' inability to execute (v. 31) ensured Jesus would die by Roman crucifixion rather than Jewish stoning, fulfilling His own prophecy of being "lifted up" (John 3:14; 8:28; 12:32-33). The word σημαίνων (sēmainōn, "signifying") indicates that the manner of death carried prophetic and typological significance — the serpent lifted up in the wilderness (Num 21:8-9).

verses.flow: Pilate therefore entered again into the Praetorium and summoned Jesus and said to Him, "Are You the King of the Jews?"
Jesus answered, "Do you say this from yourself, or did others tell you concerning Me?"
Pilate answered, "Am I a Jew? Your own nation and the chief priests delivered You to me. What have You done?"
Jesus answered, "My kingdom is not of this world. If My kingdom were of this world, My officers would be fighting, so that I would not be delivered to the Jews. But now My kingdom is not from here."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus's declaration ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμή οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ("My kingdom is not of this world") does not deny His kingship but clarifies its origin and nature. The preposition ἐκ (ek, "out of, from") indicates source, not location — His kingdom does not derive its authority from worldly systems but from the Father. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Christ's kingdom transcends all earthly dominions; He rules as the one to whom all authority in heaven and on earth is given (Dan 7:13-14; Matt 28:18).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Pilate therefore said to Him, "So then, You are a King?" Jesus answered, "You say that I am a King. For this I have been born, and for this I have come into the world, that I should bear witness to the truth. Everyone who is of the truth hears My voice."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus affirms His royal identity — σὺ λέγεις ὅτι βασιλεύς εἰμι ("You say that I am a King") — and defines His mission as bearing witness to the truth (ἀλήθεια). His kingship is exercised through truth, and His subjects are identified by their responsiveness to His voice (πᾶς ὁ ὢν ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας ἀκούει μου τῆς φωνῆς), echoing the Good Shepherd discourse (John 10:27). GREEK: The phrase εἰς τοῦτο γεγέννημαι ("for this I have been born") uses the perfect passive, pointing to His incarnation as purposeful entry into the world, not mere human origin.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Pilate says to Him, "What is truth?" And having said this, he went out again to the Jews and says to them, "I find no fault in Him.
"But there is a custom among you that I should release one to you at the Passover. Do you wish therefore that I release to you the King of the Jews?"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: They cried out therefore again, saying, "Not this one, but Barabbas!" Now Barabbas was a robber.
verses.note: GREEK: λῃστής (lēistēs, Strong's G3027) denotes not a petty thief but a bandit or insurrectionist — likely a violent rebel against Rome. THEOLOGICAL: The crowd chooses a man of violence over the Prince of Peace, a figure of worldly rebellion over the true King. The name Βαραββᾶς (Barabbas) means "son of the father" in Aramaic — a bitter irony, as the crowd releases the false "son of the father" and condemns the true Son of the Father to death.

verses.flow: Then therefore Pilate took Jesus and scourged him.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐμαστίγωσεν (emastigōsen, from mastigoō, G3146) — "scourged," denotes the severe Roman flagellum, distinct from lighter Jewish beatings. This was a brutal punishment using a multi-thonged whip embedded with bone and metal, often fatal in itself. HISTORICAL: Roman scourging typically preceded crucifixion as part of the execution protocol under Pontius Pilate's jurisdiction.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the soldiers, having plaited a crown out of thorns, placed it upon his head, and they cast around him a purple garment,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The crown of thorns (stephanos ex akanthōn) inverts the royal diadem. Thorns echo the curse of Genesis 3:18; the true King bears the curse of the ground upon his head. The purple garment (himation porphyroun) mocks his kingship while unwittingly proclaiming it. THEOLOGICAL: The soldiers parody a royal investiture—crown, robe, acclamation—yet John presents this as an ironic revelation of Jesus' true identity as cosmic King.

verses.flow: and they were coming to him and saying, "Hail, the King of the Jews!" And they were giving him strikes with the palm.
And Pilate went out again outside and says to them, "Behold, I bring him out to you, that you may know that I find no cause in him."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Then Jesus came out outside, wearing the crown of thorns and the purple garment. And he says to them, "Behold, the Man."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Behold, the Man" (Idou ho anthrōpos) — Pilate's words carry profound Christological weight beyond his intention. The phrase echoes the Adamic vocation; Jesus stands as the true Human, the last Adam (1 Cor 15:45), bearing the full weight of fallen humanity's judgment. GREEK: anthrōpos (G444) here is emphatic with the article ho, pointing to a specific, representative Man.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: When therefore the chief priests and the officers saw him, they cried out, saying, "Crucify! Crucify!" Pilate says to them, "Take him yourselves and crucify him, for I find no cause in him."
The Jews answered him, "We have a law, and according to the law he ought to die, because he made himself Son of God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The charge "he made himself Son of God" (huion theou heauton epoiēsen) reveals the real issue behind the trial. The Jewish leaders understood Jesus' claim as a claim to divine sonship in an ontological sense, not merely a messianic title (cf. John 5:18, 10:33). DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Son of God" (huios theou) in its Second Temple context could denote divine council membership, but Jesus' claim exceeded even this—he claimed unique filial identity with the Father.

verses.flow: When therefore Pilate heard this word, he was the more afraid,
and he entered into the Praetorium again and says to Jesus, "From where are you?" But Jesus gave him no answer.
Pilate therefore says to him, "To me you do not speak? Do you not know that I have authority to release you and I have authority to crucify you?"

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus answered him, "You would have no authority against me unless it had been given to you from above. Because of this, the one having delivered me to you has the greater sin."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus affirms that all earthly authority derives from above (anōthen, G509)—the same word used in John 3:3 for being born "from above." Human governance is delegated, not autonomous. The "greater sin" (meizona hamartian) belongs to the one who delivered Jesus—likely Caiaphas as representative of the priestly establishment, who bore the weight of covenantal knowledge and responsibility. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The concept of delegated authority echoes Psalm 82 and Daniel 7, where God assigns and revokes authority among rulers.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: From this, Pilate was seeking to release him. But the Jews cried out, saying, "If you release this man, you are not a friend of Caesar. Everyone who makes himself a king speaks against Caesar."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Pilate therefore, having heard these words, brought Jesus out and sat down upon the judgment seat, at a place called the Stone Pavement, but in Hebrew, Gabbatha.
Now it was the Preparation of the Passover; the hour was about the sixth. And he says to the Jews, "Behold, your King!"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "Preparation of the Passover" (paraskeuē tou pascha) indicates the day before the Passover Sabbath. The "sixth hour" in John's reckoning likely indicates noon (Roman reckoning), the very time when Passover lambs began to be slaughtered in the Temple. TYPOLOGY: Jesus, the true Paschal Lamb (1 Cor 5:7; John 1:29), is sentenced to death at the precise hour the lambs are prepared for sacrifice—John's chronology is deeply typological.

verses.flow: They therefore cried out, "Away with him! Away with him! Crucify him!" Pilate says to them, "Shall I crucify your King?" The chief priests answered, "We have no king except Caesar."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chief priests' declaration "We have no king except Caesar" (ouk echomen basilea ei mē Kaisara) is a stunning theological apostasy. Israel's confession was that God alone was their King (Judg 8:23; 1 Sam 8:7). The priestly leadership formally renounces the kingship of God to secure the death of his Anointed One. This is the ultimate covenant betrayal.

verses.flow: Then therefore he delivered him over to them that he might be crucified. They therefore took Jesus,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: and bearing his own cross, he went out to the place called Place of a Skull, which is called in Hebrew, Golgotha,
where they crucified him, and with him two others, on this side and on that side, and Jesus in the middle.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And Pilate also wrote a title and placed it on the cross. And it was written: "Jesus the Nazarene, the King of the Jews."

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: This title therefore many of the Jews read, because the place where Jesus was crucified was near the city; and it was written in Hebrew, in Latin, and in Greek.
The chief priests of the Jews therefore were saying to Pilate, "Do not write, 'The King of the Jews,' but that that one said, 'I am King of the Jews.'"
Pilate answered, "What I have written, I have written."

The soldiers therefore, when they crucified Jesus, took his garments and made four parts, to each soldier a part, and also the tunic. Now the tunic was seamless, woven from the top throughout the whole.
verses.note: GREEK: ἄραφος (araphos, G729) — "seamless," a hapax legomenon in the NT, appearing only here. The tunic (chitōn) was woven from the top (ek tōn anōthen) through the whole (di' holou). TYPOLOGY: Many patristic interpreters saw in the seamless garment an echo of the high priest's robe (Exod 28:31-32 LXX), reinforcing Jesus' role as the true High Priest offering himself as the sacrifice. The unity of the garment may also symbolize the undivided Church.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: They said therefore to one another, "Let us not tear it, but let us cast lots for it, whose it shall be," that the Scripture might be fulfilled which says, "They divided my garments among themselves, and for my clothing they cast a lot." The soldiers therefore did these things.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The direct quotation is from Psalm 22:18 (LXX 21:19), "They divided my garments among themselves, and for my clothing they cast a lot." This Psalm, which begins with the cry of dereliction (Matt 27:46), is a primary messianic passion text. John emphasizes that this happened "that the Scripture might be fulfilled" (hina hē graphē plērōthē), showing divine orchestration even through the unwitting actions of Roman soldiers.

verses.flow: And there stood beside the cross of Jesus his mother, and the sister of his mother, Mary the wife of Clopas, and Mary the Magdalene.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Jesus therefore, seeing his mother and the disciple standing nearby whom he loved, says to his mother, "Woman, behold, your son."
Then he says to the disciple, "Behold, your mother." And from that hour the disciple took her into his own home.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: After this, Jesus, knowing that all things had already been accomplished, that the Scripture might be fulfilled, says, "I thirst."
verses.note: GREEK: τετέλεσται (tetelestai, from teleō, G5055) here in the perfect passive: "all things had been accomplished." The word teleiōthē (from teleioō, G5048) rendered "might be fulfilled" carries the sense of bringing to completion or perfection. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' awareness that all things are accomplished reflects his sovereign control over the events of his passion. Even his thirst fulfills Scripture (Ps 69:21), demonstrating that nothing occurs outside the divine plan.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: A vessel full of sour wine was set there. Therefore, having placed a sponge full of the sour wine upon hyssop, they brought it to his mouth.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: When therefore Jesus received the sour wine, he said, "It is finished." And having bowed his head, he delivered up the spirit.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Τετέλεσται (Tetelestai) — "It is finished" — stands as one of the most theologically dense words in Scripture. The perfect tense indicates a completed action with abiding results. The word was used in commercial contexts for debts "paid in full." GREEK: paradidōmi (G3860) with to pneuma — "he delivered up the spirit" — implies a voluntary, sovereign act of surrender rather than mere expiration. Jesus is agent even in death (cf. John 10:18). TYPOLOGY: The completion of the Passover sacrifice is here accomplished in the true Lamb.

verses.flow: The Jews therefore, since it was the Preparation, in order that the bodies might not remain upon the cross on the Sabbath—for great was the day of that Sabbath—asked Pilate that their legs might be broken and they might be taken away.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: The soldiers therefore came, and they broke the legs of the first, and of the other who had been crucified with him.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But when they came to Jesus, as they saw that he had already died, they did not break his legs.
But one of the soldiers pierced his side with a spear, and immediately there came out blood and water.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The piercing (enuxen, from nyssō, G3572) of Jesus' side and the flow of blood and water have profound sacramental and theological significance. Blood points to the atoning sacrifice; water to the life-giving Spirit (John 7:38-39). HISTORICAL: Medically, the separation of blood and serosanguinous fluid may indicate pericardial effusion, confirming genuine death. John stresses the eyewitness nature of this testimony (v. 35), indicating its theological centrality.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the one who has seen has borne witness, and his witness is true, and that one knows that he speaks true things, that you also may believe.
For these things came to pass that the Scripture might be fulfilled: "Not a bone of him shall be broken."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Not a bone of him shall be broken" (ostoun ou syntribēsetai autou) draws from Exodus 12:46 and Numbers 9:12, the Passover lamb regulations, and possibly Psalm 34:20 (LXX 33:21). Jesus is thereby identified as the true Passover Lamb whose bones must remain unbroken. The soldiers' decision not to break his legs was a providential fulfillment of ancient Torah prescriptions concerning the sacrificial system.

verses.flow: And again another Scripture says, "They shall look upon him whom they pierced."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "They shall look upon him whom they pierced" quotes Zechariah 12:10, a passage about the eschatological outpouring of the Spirit and mourning in Jerusalem. In its original context, YHWH himself is the one pierced. John's application of this to Jesus carries profound implications for his divine identity—the pierced one on the cross is the one whom Zechariah identified with God himself. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This text connects Jesus' crucifixion to the eschatological theophany of Zechariah 12-14, where God himself comes to Jerusalem and is recognized by his people.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And after these things, Joseph of Arimathea—being a disciple of Jesus, but having been hidden on account of the fear of the Jews—asked Pilate that he might take away the body of Jesus. And Pilate gave permission. He came therefore and took away his body.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Nicodemus also came—the one who had come to him by night at the first—bearing a mixture of myrrh and aloes, about a hundred pounds.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Nicodemus (Nikodēmos, G3530), introduced in John 3:1 as a ruler of the Jews and Pharisee, now comes openly. The quantity—about a hundred litrai (approximately 75 pounds / 34 kg)—is a royal amount of burial spices, befitting the burial of a king (cf. 2 Chr 16:14). TYPOLOGY: Myrrh and aloes echo the royal wedding psalm (Ps 45:8) and the gifts of the Magi (Matt 2:11), framing Jesus' burial as a kingly anointing.

verses.flow: They therefore took the body of Jesus and bound it in linen cloths with the spices, as is the custom of the Jews to prepare for burial.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now in the place where he was crucified there was a garden, and in the garden a new tomb, in which no one had yet been laid.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The new tomb (mnēmeion kainon) in a garden (kēpos) echoes the garden of Eden. The first Adam fell in a garden; the last Adam is buried in a garden, from which he will rise. That no one had yet been laid there (oudepō oudeis ēn tetheimenos) ensures the uniqueness and unmistakability of the resurrection to follow. THEOLOGICAL: The newness of the tomb signifies that Christ's death inaugurates something entirely unprecedented in redemptive history.

verses.flow: There therefore, on account of the Preparation of the Jews, because the tomb was near, they laid Jesus.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular feminine
verb perfect passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And on the first day of the week, Mary the Magdalene comes early, while darkness yet remained, to the tomb, and she sees the stone having been taken away from the tomb.
verses.note: GREEK: "Τῇ μιᾷ τῶν σαββάτων" (tē mia tōn sabbatōn) — literally "the first of the sabbaths," a Semitic idiom for the first day of the week (Sunday). "Μνημεῖον" (mnēmeion, G3419) denotes a memorial tomb, typically a rock-hewn burial chamber. "Ἠρμένον" (ērmenon) is a perfect passive participle of "airō" (G142), indicating the stone had been taken away and remained so.

verses.flow: She runs therefore and comes to Simon Peter and to the other disciple whom Jesus loved, and says to them, "They have taken away the Lord out of the tomb, and we do not know where they have laid him."
verses.note: GREEK: "Ὃν ἐφίλει ὁ Ἰησοῦς" — "whom Jesus loved." John uses "phileō" (G5368) here rather than "agapaō," denoting affectionate, intimate love. This unnamed disciple is traditionally identified as John himself. THEOLOGICAL: Mary's plural "we do not know" (οὐκ οἴδαμεν) may suggest she was not alone initially, though John focuses his narrative on her alone.

verses.flow: Peter therefore went out, and the other disciple, and they were coming to the tomb.
And the two were running together, and the other disciple ran ahead more quickly than Peter and came first to the tomb.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And having stooped down, he sees the linen cloths lying there, yet he did not enter in.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: Simon Peter therefore comes also, following him, and he entered into the tomb, and he beholds the linen cloths lying there,

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular neuter
verb perfect passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: and the face-cloth which had been upon his head, not lying with the linen cloths, but folded up separately in one place.
verses.note: GREEK: "Σουδάριον" (soudarion, G4676) is a Latin loanword (sudarium), a cloth used to wrap the face of the dead. "Ἐντετυλιγμένον" (entetyligmenon, G1794) means "having been rolled up" or "wrapped together." THEOLOGICAL: The orderly arrangement of the burial cloths — the face-cloth folded separately — argues against theft and points to a deliberate, composed departure from the grave, consistent with resurrection rather than grave robbery.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Then therefore the other disciple also entered in, the one having come first to the tomb, and he saw and believed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "He saw and believed" (εἶδεν καὶ ἐπίστευσεν). The beloved disciple is the first to believe in the resurrection based on the evidence of the empty tomb alone, without yet seeing the risen Lord. This forms a contrast with Thomas in v.25 and anticipates the blessing of v.29.

verses.flow: For not yet did they know the Scripture, that it was necessary for him to rise from the dead.
verses.note: GREEK: "Τὴν γραφήν" (tēn graphēn, G1124) is singular — "the Scripture" — pointing to a specific prophetic text, likely Psalm 16:10 (LXX 15:10) or possibly Hosea 6:2. TYPOLOGY: The principle that Messiah "must" (δεῖ, dei, G1163) rise from the dead reflects divine necessity rooted in the prophetic Scriptures, a theme central to early apostolic preaching (Acts 2:24-31; 1 Cor 15:4).

verses.flow: The disciples therefore went away again to their own.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: But Mary stood at the tomb outside, weeping. As therefore she wept, she stooped down into the tomb,

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: and she beholds two angels in white, sitting, one at the head and one at the feet, where the body of Jesus had been lying.
And those ones say to her, "Woman, why do you weep?" She says to them, "Because they have taken away my Lord, and I do not know where they have laid him."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine
verb perfect active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Having said these things, she turned around to the things behind, and she beholds Jesus standing there, and did not know that it was Jesus.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Jesus says to her, "Woman, why do you weep? Whom do you seek?" That one, supposing that he was the gardener, says to him, "Lord, if you have carried him away, tell me where you have laid him, and I will take him away."

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Jesus says to her, "Mariam." Having turned, that one says to him in Hebrew, "Rabbouni!" (which is to say, Teacher).
verses.note: GREEK: "Ραββουνι" (Rabbouni) is an Aramaic intensive form of Rabbi, meaning "my great teacher" or "my master." John translates it for his Greek audience as "Didaskale" (Teacher). THEOLOGICAL: Jesus calls her by name — "Mariam" — echoing the shepherd motif of John 10:3: "He calls his own sheep by name." The moment of recognition through naming is profoundly personal and covenantal.

verses.flow: Jesus says to her, "Do not cling to me, for I have not yet ascended to the Father. But go to my brothers and say to them: I am ascending to my Father and your Father, and my God and your God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus distinguishes "my Father and your Father, my God and your God" rather than saying "our Father" and "our God." This preserves the unique ontological relationship of the Son to the Father while extending a covenantal familial relationship to the disciples, now called "brothers" (ἀδελφούς) for the first time. TYPOLOGY: The language echoes Ruth 1:16 and the covenant formula, now fulfilled in the new covenant community established through the resurrection. GREEK: "Μή μου ἅπτου" (mē mou haptou) — present imperative with μή indicates "stop clinging to me" or "do not keep holding onto me," not merely "do not touch me."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Mary the Magdalene comes announcing to the disciples, "I have seen the Lord!" — and that he had said these things to her.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular feminine
verb perfect passive participle genitive plural feminine

verses.flow: It being therefore evening on that day, the first of the week, and the doors having been shut where the disciples were, on account of the fear of the Jews, Jesus came and stood in the midst and says to them, "Peace to you."
verses.note: GREEK: "Εἰρήνη ὑμῖν" (Eirēnē hymin) — "Peace to you" — more than a greeting; it echoes the Hebrew "shalom" and fulfills Jesus' promise of John 14:27, "My peace I give to you." HISTORICAL: The disciples' fear of the Jewish authorities (τὸν φόβον τῶν Ἰουδαίων) reflects the real danger of association with an executed messianic claimant under Roman-occupied Judea. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' appearance through shut doors demonstrates the transformed nature of his resurrection body — physical yet transcending normal material limitations.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And having said this, he showed them his hands and his side. The disciples therefore rejoiced, having seen the Lord.
Jesus therefore said to them again, "Peace to you. As the Father has sent me, I also send you."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having said this, he breathed upon them and says to them, "Receive the Holy Spirit."
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἐνεφύσησεν" (enephysēsen, G1720) — "he breathed upon." This verb appears only here in the NT and deliberately echoes Genesis 2:7 (LXX), where God "breathed" (enephysēsen) the breath of life into Adam. TYPOLOGY: Jesus as the last Adam (1 Cor 15:45) breathes new-creation life into his disciples. This act anticipates the full outpouring at Pentecost (Acts 2) and constitutes a new-creation moment. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The giving of the Holy Spirit (πνεῦμα ἅγιον — without the article, emphasizing the Spirit's holy character and empowering presence) commissions the disciples as God's authorized agents.

verses.flow: If you forgive the sins of any, they have been forgiven them; if you retain those of any, they have been retained.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But Thomas, one of the Twelve, the one called Didymus, was not with them when Jesus came.

The other disciples therefore were saying to him, "We have seen the Lord!" But he said to them, "Unless I see in his hands the mark of the nails, and cast my finger into the mark of the nails, and cast my hand into his side, I will by no means believe."

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive plural feminine

verses.flow: And after eight days, his disciples were again inside, and Thomas was with them. Jesus comes, the doors having been shut, and stood in the midst and said, "Peace to you."
Then he says to Thomas, "Bring your finger here and see my hands, and bring your hand and cast it into my side, and do not become unbelieving, but believing."
Thomas answered and said to him, "My Lord and my God!"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Ὁ κύριός μου καὶ ὁ θεός μου" — "My Lord and my God!" This is the climactic Christological confession of the Fourth Gospel. Thomas addresses Jesus directly with the nominative articles (ὁ κύριος, ὁ θεός), making this a definite predication, not an exclamation. Jesus accepts the ascription without correction, affirming his divine identity. TYPOLOGY: The language echoes Psalm 35:23 (LXX 34:23) "my God and my Lord" and resonates with the prologue's declaration that the Word was God (John 1:1). This confession forms an inclusio with John 1:1, framing the entire Gospel.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
