verses.flow: Jesus says to him, "Because you have seen me, you have believed. Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' beatitude — "Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed" — extends beyond Thomas to all subsequent believers who come to faith through apostolic testimony rather than physical sight. This blessing bridges the narrative world of the Gospel to its readers in every age and establishes the primacy of faith grounded in testimony (cf. 1 Pet 1:8; 2 Cor 5:7).

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: Many other signs therefore Jesus also did in the presence of his disciples, which are not written in this book.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But these things have been written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that believing, you may have life in his name.
verses.note: GREEK: The textual variant between "πιστεύητε" (present subjunctive — "that you may continue believing") and "πιστεύσητε" (aorist subjunctive — "that you may come to believe") affects whether John writes primarily to sustain existing faith or to generate new faith; both purposes are theologically valid. THEOLOGICAL: This verse constitutes the original purpose statement of the Gospel. "The Christ, the Son of God" (ὁ Χριστός ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ) unites the messianic title with the divine-filial title, asserting that Jesus is both Israel's promised Anointed King and the unique Son of God. Life (ζωή, G2222) — the eternal, divine quality of life — is available "in his name," that is, in the fullness of his revealed person and authority. STRUCTURE: This verse serves as the literary conclusion to the main body of the Gospel; chapter 21 functions as an epilogue.

verses.flow: After these things Jesus manifested himself again to the disciples at the Sea of Tiberias; and he manifested himself in this way.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb phaneróō (φανερόω, Strong's G5319) means "to make visible, manifest, reveal." John uses this term rather than the Synoptic óptomai, emphasizing a deliberate self-revelation by the risen Christ. The Sea of Tiberias is an alternative Roman-era name for the Sea of Galilee (John 6:1), reflecting the city built by Herod Antipas in honor of Emperor Tiberius.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: There were together Simon Peter, and Thomas the one called Didymus, and Nathanael the one from Cana of Galilee, and the sons of Zebedee, and two others of his disciples.
Simon Peter says to them, "I am going to fish." They say to him, "We also come with you." They went out and embarked into the boat, and in that night they caught nothing.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: But when morning had already come, Jesus stood on the shore; yet the disciples did not know that it was Jesus.
Jesus then says to them, "Children, you do not have any fish, do you?" They answered him, "No."
verses.note: GREEK: Prosphágion (προσφάγιον, G4371) is a hapax legomenon in the New Testament, meaning something eaten with bread, particularly fish. The diminutive paidía ("children" or "little children") conveys tender authority, used by Jesus to address the disciples in their fruitless labor.

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Cast the net to the right side of the boat, and you will find." They cast therefore, and no longer were they able to draw it in, on account of the multitude of the fish.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: That disciple therefore whom Jesus loved says to Peter, "It is the Lord!" Simon Peter then, having heard that it is the Lord, girded on his outer garment—for he was stripped—and cast himself into the sea.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The beloved disciple perceives what the others cannot—a pattern throughout the Fourth Gospel where spiritual insight precedes action (John 20:8). Peter's immediate, impulsive response mirrors his character throughout the Gospels. The ependýtēs (ἐπενδύτης, G1903) is another rare word, occurring only here in the NT, referring to an outer garment or fisher's coat.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But the other disciples came in the small boat—for they were not far from the land, but about two hundred cubits away—dragging the net of fish.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular feminine
verb present middle participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: When therefore they came out upon the land, they see a charcoal fire laid, and fish placed upon it, and bread.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The anthrakiá (ἀνθρακιά, "charcoal fire," G439) occurs only here and in John 18:18, where Peter denied Jesus three times beside a charcoal fire. This deliberate verbal echo sets the stage for Peter's threefold restoration in vv. 15-17. Jesus as the one who prepares the meal on the shore echoes divine provision imagery (1 Kings 19:5-6; Ps 23:5).

verses.flow: Jesus says to them, "Bring some of the fish which you have now caught."

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Simon Peter therefore went up and drew the net to the land, full of great fish, one hundred fifty-three; and being so many, the net was not torn.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The number 153 has generated extensive discussion. Jerome noted that ancient zoologists (such as Oppian) catalogued 153 species of fish, potentially symbolizing the universal scope of the apostolic mission. Others note it is the triangular number of 17 (the sum of integers 1-17). The unbroken net may contrast with the torn nets of Luke 5:6, signifying the eschatological unity of God's gathered people.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Jesus says to them, "Come, dine." And none of the disciples dared to examine him, "Who are you?"—knowing that it is the Lord.
Jesus comes and takes the bread and gives it to them, and the fish likewise.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: This was now the third time Jesus was manifested to the disciples, having been raised from the dead.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: John explicitly numbers this as the third post-resurrection appearance to the disciples as a group (after John 20:19-23 and 20:26-29). The verb phaneróō is used in the passive (ἐφανερώθη), indicating that Christ's resurrection appearances are acts of divine self-revelation, not mere chance encounters.

verses.flow: When therefore they had dined, Jesus says to Simon Peter, "Simon, son of John, do you love me more than these?" He says to him, "Yes, Lord, you know that I have affection for you." He says to him, "Feed my lambs."
verses.note: GREEK: The famous interchange between agapáō (ἀγαπάω, G25) and philéō (φιλέω, G5368) has been debated extensively. Jesus asks with agapáō ("do you love me with self-giving love?"), while Peter responds with philéō ("I have affection for you"). Some scholars argue John uses these as synonyms for stylistic variation; others see a deliberate theological distinction. The threefold question mirrors Peter's threefold denial (John 18:17, 25, 27). TYPOLOGY: The command "Feed my lambs" echoes the shepherd-king typology of Ezekiel 34 and Psalm 23, with Jesus as the Chief Shepherd commissioning under-shepherds.

verses.flow: He says to him again a second time, "Simon, son of John, do you love me?" He says to him, "Yes, Lord, you know that I have affection for you." He says to him, "Shepherd my sheep."

He says to him the third time, "Simon, son of John, do you have affection for me?" Peter was grieved because he said to him the third time, "Do you have affection for me?" And he said to him, "Lord, you know all things; you know that I have affection for you." Jesus says to him, "Feed my sheep."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: On the third question, Jesus shifts to philéō—descending, as it were, to Peter's own word—and Peter is grieved. The progression from arnía ("lambs") to próbata ("sheep") and the alternation between bóskō ("feed") and poimaínō ("shepherd/tend") suggests a comprehensive pastoral commission encompassing nurture, guidance, and protection. Peter's appeal to Christ's omniscience ("you know all things") is both a confession of inadequacy and a declaration of faith.

verses.flow: Truly, truly, I say to you, when you were younger, you girded yourself and walked where you wished; but when you grow old, you will stretch out your hands, and another will gird you and carry you where you do not wish."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "amen, amen" formula (ἀμὴν ἀμήν) is unique to the Fourth Gospel and marks solemn prophetic declaration. The stretching out of hands (ἐκτενεῖς τὰς χεῖράς σου) is widely understood as a reference to crucifixion. Early church tradition (Clement of Rome, 1 Clement 5:4; Eusebius, H.E. 3.1) records that Peter was indeed crucified in Rome under Nero. TYPOLOGY: Peter's death "glorifying God" (δοξάσει τὸν θεόν) parallels Jesus' own glorification through death (John 12:23-33; 13:31-32).

verses.flow: Peter, having turned around, sees the disciple whom Jesus loved following—the one who also leaned back upon his breast at the supper and said, "Lord, who is the one betraying you?"

verses.flow: Peter therefore, seeing this one, says to Jesus, "Lord, and what about this one?"
Jesus says to him, "If I wish him to remain until I come, what is that to you? You follow me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' conditional statement ("If I wish him to remain until I come") is neither a promise nor a prophecy about the beloved disciple's immortality, as v. 23 immediately clarifies. The phrase "until I come" (héōs érchomai) employs the language of the Parousia (1 Cor 11:26; Rev 22:20). Jesus asserts sovereign authority over the destinies of both disciples, redirecting Peter from comparison to personal obedience.

verses.flow: This saying therefore went out among the brothers, that that disciple does not die. Yet Jesus did not say to him that he does not die, but, "If I wish him to remain until I come, what is that to you?"

verses.flow: This is the disciple who bears witness concerning these things, and who wrote these things; and we know that his testimony is true.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: This verse constitutes the colophon of the Fourth Gospel. The shift to first person plural ("we know") likely represents the community associated with the beloved disciple, attesting to his authorial role and the reliability of his testimony. The phrase "the one who wrote these things" (ho grápsas taûta) establishes apostolic eyewitness authorship as the foundation of the Gospel's authority (cf. John 19:35; 1 John 1:1-4).

verses.flow: And there are also many other things which Jesus did, which if they were written one by one, I suppose not even the world itself would have room for the books being written.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb oîmai (οἶμαι, "I suppose") is a rare personal intrusion by the author/editor, occurring only here in John's Gospel. The hyperbolic closing—that the world itself could not contain the books—employs a recognized rhetorical convention in ancient literature (cf. Ecclesiastes 12:12). It magnifies the inexhaustible nature of Christ's works and serves as a fitting conclusion to a Gospel whose prologue declared him the eternal Logos through whom all things were made (John 1:3).
